According to Fu Liang's compilation, Zhu Changshu has been mentioned in previous volumes, but the rest are not verifiable.

Du Yuan, courtesy name Yongping, was from Fuchong, Zitong. His family was very wealthy. He had a son named Tianbao, whom he deeply loved. At the age of ten, Tianbao died suddenly of an illness in the third year of Taiyuan (378 AD). Several months later, a pig in the family gave birth to five piglets, one of which was particularly fat. After the new official arrived, Du Yuan planned to use this pig as a gift. Just as he was about to catch and kill it, a Buddhist monk suddenly appeared before him and said, "This pig is your son. Do you not remember him after more than a hundred days?" After speaking, the monk suddenly disappeared. Looking around, Du Yuan saw the monk soar into the sky and depart to the West. The fragrance spread and lasted for a whole day before dissipating. This story is recorded in the "Pearl Forest" (珠林) series, chapter fifty-two.

During the reign of Emperor Taiyuan in the Eastern Jin Dynasty, Tang Zun of Shangyu had a sudden and severe illness and died. After a night, he regained consciousness and said that someone called him away, leading him to a city or administrative center, but he did not enter. Soon, he saw his maternal uncle or cousin coming out of the city and asked him in surprise, "Why are you here?" Tang Zun replied, "I have been away from my aunt and sister for many years and wanted to visit them. I originally planned to depart, but I saw several people at night who urgently called me here. I could return immediately but did not know the way back." The maternal uncle or cousin said, "Your aunt has been dead for two years. Your eldest sister's son, Dao Wen, was recently taken away. He was released as a favor but stayed to watch a performance. He did not immediately return home and returned after many days. The family had already performed the burial rites. He entered the coffin and shook the coffin lid, hoping his family would realize and open the coffin. The coffin reached the road and fell under a wooden cart. The family considered opening the coffin but consulted a diviner who said it was inauspicious, so they did not dare to open it. Dao Wen could not be revived. He is now engaged in hard labor in hell, working extremely hard. You should leave quickly and not stay here. Your younger sister has also died. You and your aunt are now in hell, suffering daily. I do not know when you can be freed from hell. You should return and tell Dao Wen to diligently perform good deeds, perhaps he can escape from hell." At this point, the way back was shown to Tang Zun.

As they were about to part, he entrusted Zun with these words: "You are able to return to life, which is indeed a great cause for celebration. Life in the world is short, fleeting like dust in the wind. Heaven and hell, the consequences of good and bad actions; I heard about it before, and now I see it in reality. You should diligently cultivate good deeds and be filial and respectful. Receive the teachings and keep the precepts, be cautious not to violate them. Once you leave the human body, you will enter this realm of sin. Endless suffering and cruelty, regretting it afterward will be too late. Diligence should be in your heart, do not neglect it. My family members, who did not believe in karma in life, are now suffering greatly, enduring long-lasting pain, burning and hurting, with no moment of respite. If they want to change from evil to good for even a day, what can they do? Knowing all this, I entrust you with this. Persuade and guide your family, encourage each other." After speaking, he wept, and they parted ways. Zun returned home along the road, soon reaching his house. The family was preparing the coffin, just about to perform the funeral rites. Zun touched the body, and soon the body regained breath. After a few days, he gradually recovered. He persuaded and showed his relatives and acquaintances, all following the great teachings. Initially, Zun's aunt in Nanjun was Xu Han, his elder sister in Jiangxia was Le Yu Yu, and his younger sister in Wuxing was Yan Wan. The distance was far, and communication had been cut off for a long time. After Zun recovered, he went to the three prefectures to find his aunt and younger sister. His sister's children had indeed died. His elder sister also said that after her son's burial, the coffin moved and fell from the cart, just as his uncle had said. After hearing Zun's explanation of his brother's sudden death, his sister felt even more grief and anger, and wore mourning clothes again. *Zhu Jin* (Pearl Forest), volume 97.

Xie Fu, courtesy name Qing Xu, was from Shan Yin, Kuaiji, and was the nephew of General of the Army You. From a young age, he possessed noble character and retired to the East Mountain, firmly believing in the Great Law and diligently practicing without tiring. He personally wrote the *Shurangama Sutra*. While staying at the White Horse Temple in the capital, the temple was engulfed by a fire disaster, and all the belongings and other scriptures were reduced to ashes. However, this scripture was only burned at the edges of the paper, leaving the text completely preserved with no damage or loss. When he died, his friends suspected that he had attained enlightenment. Upon hearing of this scripture, they were even more amazed. In the eighth year of the Yungang era (431 AD), a great fire disaster struck the city of Pu'an in Hedong (modern-day Yongji, Shanxi). The fire crossed the river as if flying, and could not be extinguished. All the military outposts, civilian houses, and everything else were completely destroyed. Only the monasteries and pagodas were spared from the fire. Small houses in the neighborhood, if they contained scriptures or images, were also largely spared. Sometimes even if a house was burned down, the scriptures within were still found intact, with the paper looking as good as new. The entire city was amazed, and people began to revere and believe in Buddhism. This is recorded in the 18th chapter of the *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest).

Ding Cheng of Jinyin, styled De Shen, was the magistrate of Jinyin during the Jian'an period (196–220 AD). According to the Jin dynasty chronology, there is doubt whether it should be Jian'an or Jianyuan. At that time, a woman from the northern border of the county went to an outer well to draw water. A man with a long nose and deep-set eyes passed by the well and asked the woman for a drink. After drinking, he suddenly disappeared. The woman then felt abdominal pain, which intensified. She cried out for a while, and then suddenly sat up, speaking in a foreign language and gesturing. Several dozen households in the village gathered to watch. The woman called for paper and ink, wanting to write something. When she got the pen, she wrote in a foreign script: horizontal lines, sometimes resembling the character "乙," sometimes resembling "己." She filled five sheets of paper, threw them on the ground, and instructed people to read the text. No one in the village could read it. There was a boy, about ten years old, whom the woman pointed out as being able to read it. The boy took the paper and read it in the foreign language. The onlookers were amazed and did not understand what it meant. The woman called the boy to stand up and perform a ritual. The boy stood up, raised his foot, and used his hands to make some gestures. After a short while, they all stopped. The woman immediately reported this to Ding Cheng. Ding Cheng summoned the woman and the boy and asked them about it. They said that they had been unaware at the time but knew that Ding Cheng wanted to verify the incident. Ding Cheng then sent an official with the text to a temple in Xu (许), to show it to an old man from the foreign land. The old man was greatly surprised and said that the text resembled a lost Buddhist scripture, which had been lost due to the long distance and was feared to be unobtainable. Although he could recite it, it was not complete. This was indeed the original text, and he decided to copy it down. This story is recorded in Chapter Eighteen of *Zhu Lin* (珠林).

Wang Ningzhi, Prince of Langya, was married to the daughter of General Xie Yi, a left general of the Jin dynasty. He often experienced the loss of family members, and his two sons mourned excessively, crying for many years as if facing the greatest hardship. Later, he suddenly saw his two sons return together, both wearing shackles and chains. They comforted and encouraged their mother, advising her to be self-reliant and cut ties. They said that they both had sins, and if she showed compassion, she could perform good deeds for them. As a result, his grief gradually subsided, and he became diligent in performing good deeds and cultivating virtue. (From the *Jade Forest*, Volume 33) Zhi Dun, a Buddhist monk of the Jin dynasty, was from Chenliu and was known by the name Dao Lin. He had a divine presence and sharp intellect, making him a leading figure in the fusion of Taoist and Buddhist thought. He often debated with his teacher about the moral status of various creatures. Zhi Dun argued that eggs were not yet fully developed and thus should not be killed, unlike small insects, which deserved different treatment. Soon after, his teacher passed away. One day, Zhi Dun suddenly saw his teacher appear before him, holding an egg and throwing it to the ground. The egg broke, and a chick emerged, walking out of its shell. Zhi Dun immediately realized his mistake and regretted his earlier words. Soon after, both his teacher and the chick disappeared. (From the *Jade Forest*, Volume 72)

During the Jin Dynasty, the seven ridges of Mount Lu converged to the east, forming a series of peaks. The cliffs were so steep and rugged that no one could climb them. In the Taiyuan period, Fan Ning, the governor of Yuzhang, planned to build a school and sent people to gather materials from the mountain. They saw a man dressed in a monk's robe ascending into the void straight up. Upon reaching the peak, he turned his body and sat upon it. After a while, he raised clouds and mist, and then disappeared. At that time, several people who were gathering herbs all witnessed this. Those skilled in writing were all inspired by it. The Buddhist monk Tan Di wrote in his *Lu Mountain Assignment*: "The Bodhisattva ascended into the clouds and sat on the peak, disappearing into the void." In the Jin Dynasty, the Buddhist monk Sang Lang was known for his strict discipline and was respected by both Han and foreign people. Once, while traveling with a few companions, he suddenly told his fellow travelers, "You left your belongings in the temple, and it seems someone has stolen them." They immediately returned and indeed caught the thief. During the Taiyuan period, he built a pagoda and temple in the Jinju Valley of Fenggao County, creating statues and images. In the late period of Former Qin, when the emperor Fu Jian suppressed Buddhism, he alone respected the group led by Sang Lang and did not dare to destroy their temple. At that time, both monks and laypeople revered him, and he could predict the number of visitors before they arrived, always preparing accordingly. Before the temple was built, the valley was often plagued by tigers that caused harm. After the temple was established, the tigers became like domesticated animals. The Xianbei ruler Murong De used the taxes from two counties to support his court. To this day, the valley is known as Lang Gong Valley.

The Buddhist monk named Fashang (释法相) was from Hedong (河东). He often lived alone in the mountains, devoted to rigorous practice. Birds and animals gathered around him, tame like domestic animals. There was a large stone box at the Temple of Mount Tai, used to hold treasures. Fashang was traveling in the mountains and stayed in the temple. He saw a person dressed in a black robe and wearing a military hat, who ordered Fashang to open the box. When the person spoke, he disappeared. The stone lid of the box was extremely heavy, but Fashang managed to open it effortlessly. He then took the treasures and distributed them to the poor. Later, he crossed the river to the south and lived in Yuecheng Temple. Suddenly, he became carefree and unrestrained, skilled in jest and humor. Sometimes he went naked, offending the nobility. The Northern General Military Commander Sima Tian disliked his lack of restraint and invited him, then poisoned him. Fashang drank three cups repeatedly, but his spirit remained clear and calm, as if nothing had happened. He was aged 89 and died at the end of the Yuanxing era. This story is recorded in the "Jewel Forest" (珠林十九).

Zhang Chong of Jin, a native of Duling, Jingzhao. When he was young, he was devoted to the law. During the Taiyuan period of the Eastern Jin, after Fu Jian was defeated, thousands of families from Chang'an fled south to return to the Jin dynasty. They were detained by the local garrison and considered as bandits. The men were killed, and the women and children were captured. Zhang Chong and four others were chained together by hands and feet, neck and body dug into pits, and buried up to the waist, each separated by twenty steps. The next day, they were to be chased and shot with arrows for entertainment. Zhang Chong, feeling desperate, focused solely on the Bodhisattva Guanyin. At night, the chains suddenly broke, allowing them to free themselves. They then escaped and managed to escape. Zhang Chong, now in pain from his feet, continued on the road with his companions, passing by a temple. There, he again called out the name of the Bodhisattva Guanyin, prostrated himself with sincere devotion, placed a stone in front of him, and made a vow, saying that he now intended to cross to the east of the river, to appeal to the Jin emperor about the chaos, to seek justice for these wronged spirits, and to save his wife and children. If his wish was granted, the stone would split into two. After Zhang Chong finished his prostration, the stone indeed split. Zhang Chong then arrived in the capital, reported to the White Tiger Hall, and detailed the list of wronged individuals. The emperor then pardoned them all. Those who had been sold as slaves were registered as commoners. The monk Zhi Sheng, who witnessed this with his own eyes, recorded it in the 65th volume of the "Flower Garden of the Dharma Treasury."

晋王懿，字仲德，太原人也。守车骑将军。世信奉法。父苗，符坚时为中山太守，为丁零所害。仲德与兄元德，携母南归。登陟峭崄，饥疲绝粮。无复余计，惟归心三宝。忽见一童子，牵青牛，见懿等饥，各乞一饭。因忽不见。时积雨大水，懿前望浩然，不知何处为浅，可得揭蹑。俄有一白狼，旋绕其前，过水而反，似若引导。如此者三。于是逐狼而渡，水才至膝。俄得陆路，南归晋帝。后自五兵尚书，为徐州刺史。尝欲设斋：宿昔洒扫，敷陈香华，盛列经像。忽闻法堂有经呗声，清婉流畅。懿遽往观：见有五沙门在佛坐前，威容伟异，神仪秀出。懿知非凡僧，心甚欢敬。沙门回相瞻?，意若依然。音旨未交，忽而竦身飞空而去。亲表宾僚，见者甚众。咸悉欣跃，倍增信悟。珠林六十五 Jin Wang Yi, courtesy name Zhongde, was from Taiyuan. He served as the Guardian of the Cavalry General. His family had always believed in and followed the law. His father, Miao, was the governor of Zhongshan during the reign of Fu Jian, but was killed by the Tiele. Zhongde and his elder brother Yuande took their mother southward. They ascended steep and dangerous terrain, starving, exhausted, and without food. With no other options, they dedicated themselves to the Three Jewels of Buddhism. Suddenly, they saw a boy leading a green ox, who saw their hunger and asked for a single meal for each of them. The boy then vanished. At that time, there was heavy rain and flooding. Yi looked ahead and saw the vast waters, unsure where the shallow part was where one could wade across. Soon, a white wolf circled in front of him, crossed the water, and returned, as if guiding them. This happened three times. Following the wolf, they crossed the water, which only reached their knees. Soon, they found a land route and returned south to the Jin Emperor. Later, he served as a military official and became the governor of Xuzhou. Once, he intended to set up a vegetarian meal: he cleaned and prepared the space, arranged incense and flowers, and displayed scriptures and images. Suddenly, he heard chanting in the lecture hall, clear, graceful, and flowing. Yi hurried to see and found five monks before the Buddha's seat, with majestic and extraordinary appearance and demeanor. Yi knew they were not ordinary monks and was very happy and respectful. The monks looked back at each other, as if they recognized something. Before they could exchange words, they suddenly rose up and flew away into the sky. Relatives, friends, and guests, many of whom witnessed this, were all delighted and greatly increased their faith and understanding. Chapter 65 of the "Jeweled Forest."

Jin Cheng Daohui, courtesy name Wenhua, was from Wuchang. His family had long practiced the Five Sheng Daoist sect and did not believe in Buddhism. He often said, "Since ancient times, the true path has never surpassed that of Laozi. Why believe in and be misled by foreign words, thinking they are superior teachings?" In the 15th year of Taiyuan (390 AD), he fell ill and died. His heart was still warm, and his family did not perform the burial rites immediately. After several days, he revived and recounted his experience. He said that when he first died, he saw about ten people binding and leading him away. He encountered a monk who said that this person had accumulated good karma and should not be bound. The monk then released him and sent him away. The road was smooth, but on both sides were dense thorns, barely allowing passage. The sinners were forced to run through this thorny path, their flesh sticking to the thorns, and their cries and groans filled the air. Seeing Daohui walking on a peaceful road, they all admired and said, "The path of the Buddhist disciple is indeed better than ours!" Daohui replied, "I do not follow the Dharma." The monk smiled and said, "You have just forgotten."

Hui recalled his past life serving the Buddha, having undergone five cycles of rebirth and death. He had forgotten his original aspiration. In this life, at a young age, he encountered evil people, failed to distinguish between right and wrong, and was misled by false teachings. Upon arriving in a large city, he proceeded directly to the hearing chamber. There, he saw a man, around forty or fifty years old, sitting facing south. The man was surprised and asked, “You should not be here.” Another person, wearing a plain robe and headband, holding records, replied, “This person cut down a sacred tree and committed murder, so he should be here.” The monk whom Hui had encountered earlier also followed him inside and argued persuasively. He claimed that cutting down the tree was not a crime. This person had accumulated much merit in past lives, and though the act of murder was grave, the retribution had not yet arrived. The man sitting facing south said, “It is possible to punish the person who recorded this,” and ordered Hui to sit. He then apologized, saying, “The minor demon made a mistake, wrongfully bringing you here. It is also because you forgot your past life, not knowing to follow the great orthodox teachings.” He was about to send Hui back, and was then appointed temporarily as a deputy inspector general.

After observing the hells, Hui happily declined and left, guided and followed. They traveled through various cities, each of which was a hell. There were countless people, all suffering punishments. They saw tugging dogs, biting people's joints, causing muscles to scatter and blood to cover the ground. There were also flocks of birds with beaks like blades, flying swiftly, their blood flowing into people's mouths, from inside out, through and through. The people twisted and called out, their bones and tendons broken and falling. Other things seen were generally similar to Zhao Tai's experiences, not fully recorded. Only these two scenes were different, so they are recorded in detail. After observing all the hells, Hui was then sent back. Again, he saw the monk he had met before, who gave him a copper object shaped like a small bell. The monk said, "When you return home, you can throw this outside the door, do not bring it into the house. On a certain day in the year, you will face danger. Be cautious beyond this point, your life will extend to ninety years."

A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. Dao Hui once lived in the southern part of the capital city's main street. One day, he returned home and reached the soapberry tree bridge. There, he encountered three relatives who stopped their carriage to talk and mourn his death. When he arrived at the door, he saw a maid walking and crying while going to market. Neither the people nor the maid were seen by anyone else. As Dao Hui was about to enter, he placed copper objects outside the door on a tree. The light spread and rose into the sky, gradually becoming smaller and suddenly disappearing. When he reached the door, he smelled the stench of the corpse and felt sad and disgusted. At that time, guests and relatives came to mourn, and many people rushed to Dao Hui, preventing him from lingering. Because he entered the room with the body, he suddenly revived. He described the people he met in the carriage and the maid at the market, and they all matched what he had seen. Later, Dao Hui became a prosecutor and participated in hearing cases in the west hall. Before he could take his place, he suddenly felt dizzy and disoriented, not recognizing anyone, and recovered after half a day. Calculating the time, it was the period Dao Hui had been warned about. Shortly afterward, he was promoted to the position of Governor of Guangzhou. He died in the sixth year of the Yüan Jia era, at the age of sixty-nine. This story is recorded in *Zhu Lin* Volume 55.

During the Jin Dynasty, there was a Buddhist monk named Huìdá, whose surname was Liu and given name was Sàhé. He was from Líshí, Xīhé. Before he became a monk, he was experienced in military service; he had not yet heard of Buddhist teachings; he valued martial virtues; and he loved hunting. At the age of thirty-one, he suddenly died from an illness. His body was still warm, and his family had not yet performed the burial rites. Seven days later, he revived. He recounted that as he was nearing death, he saw two people binding and taking him away. They headed northwest. The path became higher, gradually reaching a flat road. Trees lined both sides. He saw a person holding a bow and wearing a sword, standing in the middle of the road. The person pointed to two others and took Huìdá to the west. He saw many houses with white walls and red columns. He entered one house where a beautifully dressed woman was. He asked for food. A voice from the air said, "Do not give it to him." Someone emerged from the ground, holding an iron pestle, and was about to strike him. Huìdá ran quickly, entering ten or so houses, but finally got nothing. He then saw an old woman riding in a carriage, who gave him a scroll. He accepted it. He reached a house to the west, with elegant and well-ordered buildings. An old woman was sitting outside the house, with tiger teeth in her mouth. The interior had beautifully decorated beds and curtains, a bamboo mat, and a green table. There was another woman there. She asked Huìdá, "Did you receive the book?" He gave the scroll to her. The woman took the remaining books and compared them. Soon, he saw two monks, who said to Huìdá, "Do you recognize us?" He replied, "I do not recognize you." The monks said, "Now it is appropriate to devote oneself to the Buddha Śākyamuni." Huìdá followed the words and made a vow, and then followed the monks together. He saw a city in the distance, similar to the city of Chang'an, but the color was very dark, and it was a city of iron. He saw people with very tall and large bodies, their skin black like lacquer, and their hair dragging on the ground.

The monk said, "This is a ghost from the underworld." The place was extremely cold. There were ice shards as large as mats, flying and scattering onto people. When they hit someone's head, the head would break off; when they hit someone's feet, the feet would break off. The two monks said, "This is the Cold Ice Hell." The monk immediately recognized his past life, knowing that the two monks were disciples of the same teacher during the time of the Buddha Vipasyin. As a novice, he had committed a lay sin and thus could not receive ordination. Despite the presence of the Buddha, he never had the opportunity to follow him. He has been reborn twice: once in Qiang and now in Jin. He also saw his uncle in this hell. The uncle said to the monk, "In the past, when I was in Ye, I did not know about Buddhism. I saw someone pouring water over a statue and tried it myself; but I did not return the water. Now I am suffering punishment. I still have the merit from pouring water and will be reborn in heaven." Next, the monk saw the Blade Mountain Hell. He experienced various hells in sequence, seeing many things. Each hell was in a different city and did not mix with others. The number of people in the hells was as numerous as sand and could not be counted. The punishments and rules in the hells were roughly consistent with what is said in the scriptures. As the monk experienced the hells firsthand, there was a glimmer of light. Suddenly, he saw a golden light that was bright and clear. He saw a person who was about two zhang tall, with a beautiful appearance and a golden body. Those around him said, "This is the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara." Everyone rose to greet and pay respects. There were two monks who looked similar and walked east together. After the monk paid his respects, the Bodhisattva gave a detailed explanation, speaking for more than a thousand words, concluding, "In setting up merit for the deceased, whether they are parents, siblings, or even friends and passersby, it is beneficial. Whether in a temple or at home, the deceased can be freed from suffering. On the 15th day of the seventh month, when monks receive their year-end offerings, setting up offerings at this time is especially meritorious."

If one makes objects to serve as offerings, each object should be marked with an inscription stating that it is personally presented by a certain person to the Triple Gem — the more merit is given, the swifter the reward. Monks and laypeople, who see their own bodies as sources of transgression, and who recognize the sins of past lives and various evil deeds, if they are able to fully confess in public, without losing the order of events, and who sincerely and diligently repent, their sins are immediately destroyed. If, however, someone is too shy or ashamed to confess before the assembly, they may confess in private, quietly recalling and confessing their faults, without losing the sequence of events — in that case too, their sins are also erased. If something is unintentionally omitted and not deliberately concealed, though they cannot be entirely exempt, their retribution will be somewhat lighter. But if one cannot repent and lacks a sense of shame or remorse, this is called clinging to one’s faults without turning back — after death, they will certainly fall into hell. Furthermore, if others build pagodas or temple halls, even if it is just one piece of earth or one piece of wood, whether dyed or green, if they offer it with sincerity and earnestness, they will gain great merit.

If you see a pagoda or temple hall, and there is grass or weeds, and it is not weeded or cleared, and you step on it and walk, and perform acts of worship or merit, the merit will immediately be exhausted." He then said, "The scriptures are revered classics, the means of transformation and guidance. The Perfection of Wisdom Sutra has the supreme merit. The Sutra of the Lion's Roar is second-best. If there is a good person, where they read and recite the scriptures, the ground becomes diamond-like, but ordinary beings with physical eyes cannot see it. If they can diligently recite and uphold, they will not fall into hell. The definitive edition of the Prajnaparamita Sutra, and the Buddha's alms bowl, will later come to the Han region (China). If one performs a good deed in connection with this scripture and alms bowl, they will receive a reward and be reborn in heaven, gaining double the merit." What was said is extensive, but a brief summary is provided. The speaker leaves and says, "You should endure many lifetimes, suffering severe karmic consequences. Because you have heard the teachings of the scriptures and developed a joyful heart, now you will experience a lighter karmic consequence. After one cycle, you will be free. You will be saved and live, and can become a monk.

There are five places that all have the Ashoka Stupa: Luoyang, Linzi, Jianye, Maoyin, and Chengdu. Additionally, there are two stone statues in Wu, created by the ghosts and spirits commanded by Emperor Ashoka. These statues are quite true to life. Those who go to worship them will not fall into hell. He then traveled eastward. He made a bow and left. The southern main road was wide over a hundred steps. The number of people walking on the road was incalculable. On both sides of the road, many monks were lined up. A person holding a brush stood facing north and said to He, "When you were in Xiangyang, why did you kill the deer?" He knelt and answered, "Someone else shot the deer, and I just added a wound. I didn't eat the meat, so why should I be punished?" At that moment, He saw the place in Xiangyang where the deer was killed, with grass, trees, mountains, and ravines suddenly filling his vision. The black horse he was riding could also speak, and all confirmed the year, month, day, and hour when He killed the deer.

The man was greatly frightened and had no response. After a moment, someone brought something (unclear) to him and threw him into a cauldron of boiling water. He looked at his body, which was rotten and shattered. There was a wind that blew him, gathering him at a small shore. Suddenly, without realizing it, he returned to his complete form. The recorder asked again: "You also shot a pheasant, and also killed a goose." After he finished speaking, he was thrown into the cauldron of boiling water again, as before, in the same way of rotting. After receiving this retribution, they sent him away. He entered a large city where people lived. They told him: "You have received a light punishment, and have been reborn, this is due to the power of good fortune. From now on, will you commit more sins?" They then sent someone to send him. He saw his old body from afar and did not want to return. The person who was sending him pushed and pulled, and after a long time, he attached to his form and revived. He diligently followed the law and then immediately left home to become a monk. His name was Huidat. At the end of the Taoyuan period, he was still in the capital. Later, he went to Xuchang, and no one knows what happened to him. Zhu Lin 86.

During the Yuanxing period of the Jin dynasty, there was a monk named Zhi Fachuang, who served as the abbot of Xianyi Temple in Shanyin. While constructing a temple wall, he went to buy wood on the shore of Lan. On the way, they passed through a lake area, and the owner of the wood was a woman. They agreed to meet at the wood site and set the price. So, they went together on the same boat. Once they entered the large lake, a violent storm struck at dusk, with waves like mountains. The monk's small boat was flooded, and his life was in danger at any moment. He thought that he had no good fortune and suddenly encountered this disaster. Additionally, he was traveling with the woman, which greatly increased his fear. He then recited the Sutra of the Bodhisattva Guanyin with all his heart. Soon, a large boat appeared, floating toward him. At that time, it was already night, and there were no other travelers. Reflecting to himself, the monk wondered why there should have been such a flowing boat, suspecting it was a divine power. They then crossed together and boarded it. But the small boat immediately sank. The large boat followed the waves and moved, and soon reached the shore. This story is recorded in the *Jade Forest*, volume seventeen.

A Buddhist monk named Sishikaikai from the Eastern Jin Dynasty, during the second year of the Long'an era, climbed a ridge to gather licorice when he was captured by the Qiang people. That year was marked by a severe famine, and the Qiang and Hu people were so desperate that they resorted to eating each other. He was taken into a stockade, where he was to be eaten along with ten or so others who were already confined there. The Qiang people prepared meals daily, but only Sishikaikai survived. From the moment he was captured, he secretly recited the *Guanyin Sutra* without ceasing in his heart. On the day he was scheduled to be eaten, just as dawn began to break, a large tiger suddenly appeared, roaring angrily at the Qiang people. Terrified, they scattered and fled. The tiger then approached the wooden fence, biting it until a small opening was created, large enough for a person to pass through. Afterward, the tiger slowly left. Initially, Sishikaikai thought the tiger would attack him, but when the fence was breached and the tiger did not enter, he began to suspect that something unusual was happening. He believed it was the power of Guanyin. Calculating that the Qiang people would not return immediately, he broke through the fence and fled, traveling at night and hiding during the day, and ultimately escaped. This account is recorded in the *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Grove), chapter seventeen.

Pan Daoxiu of Wu County, during the Jin dynasty, was in his twenties when he served as a military officer and was dispatched to the north. According to the *Guangji* (Miscellaneous Records), the original text may have an error, and the correct version is: "He once followed the army north and the army suffered a small defeat." Pan Daoxiu fled and was captured, and he was enslaved in several places. Captured in a foreign land, he had no way to return home. He had faith in Buddhism and constantly prayed to Guanyin. In his dreams, he often saw images of Guanyin. Later, he fled south and became lost. In a remote mountain, he suddenly encountered a true image of Guanyin, as if it were a moving statue. He performed a ritual of worship. After the ritual was completed, he suddenly realized that the image had disappeared. According to the *Guangji*, an alternative reading is: "He suddenly realized that he was safe and found his way home, thus returning to his native land." After this, he became even more devout. He died at nearly sixty years old. Recorded in *Zhulin* 17 and *Guangji* 110. Luang Gou, according to the *Guangji*, whose name is also written as Luan Gou, was an unknown person who, in his youth, practiced the Dharma. He once served as the magistrate of Fuping and was dispatched to the south to suppress Lu Xun. He suffered a small defeat, and his ships were nearly destroyed by fire, while the enemy was also closing in. In the middle of the river, the wind and waves were terrifying, and Luang Gou was filled with fear. He continued to recite the name of Guanyin. Suddenly, he saw a person standing alone in the middle of the river, waist-deep in water. Luang Gou knew that his prayers had been answered. The fire and the enemy were now at bay. He jumped into the water to join the person. His body floated and rose, and his feet touched the ground. Soon after, the main army sent ships to rescue the defeated soldiers, and he was saved. Recorded in *Zhulin* 17 and *Guangji* 110.

During the Jin dynasty, there was a monk named Fa Zhi, who was once a layperson. He often traveled alone and one day found himself in a marsh, where he suddenly encountered a fierce fire that erupted from all directions, blocking all escape routes. In his desperate situation, he sincerely recited and chanted the name of Guanyin. Suddenly, the fire passed, and all the grass in the marsh was completely burned, except for the spot where Fa Zhi stood, which remained unscathed. Thus, he began to revere and follow the great law (Dharma). Later, he became a general under Emperor Yao Xing and followed him to fight against the Xianbei. When the army retreated, he lost his horse and was captured. He hid in a thorny bush by a ditch, managed to cover his head, and continued to recite the name of Guanyin with a very sincere and earnest heart. People on the other side of the ditch called out to the rear army to execute him, but when the army passed and searched, they could not find him. He was saved from afar. Later, he became a monk. According to the *Zhulin* (Jade Forest) and *Guangji* (Extensive Records), volume 17, page 110. In the Jin dynasty, there was a layperson named Nan Gong Ziao from Shiping. He was garrisoning at Xiping City and was defeated by the Longle Prince of the Fofo (Xianbei) tribe. Thousands of people in the city were all killed. Although Nan Gong Ziao knew he would surely die, he still sincerely recited the name of Guanyin. Later, it was his turn, and multiple swords came down, some high, some low, or some off-target. The person holding the knife was tired and his limbs did not follow his commands. At that time, the Longle Prince personally oversaw the execution and asked him in surprise. Nan Gong Ziao casually replied, "Can make a saddle." Then he was spared. Nan Gong Ziao did not know why he said this. Later, he managed to escape. He made a small statue of Guanyin and kept it in a fragrant box, carrying it on his head when traveling. According to the *Guangji* (Extensive Records), volume 17, page 110.

Jin Dynasty Liu Du, from Liaocheng in the Plain (a region in modern-day Shandong), lived in a village with over a thousand households, all of whom followed the great law, created images, and provided support for monks and nuns. During the time of the foreign ruler "Mumu," this county had some fugitives, and the ruler "未" became extremely angry, wanting to completely destroy the city. The people were all terrified and believed they would inevitably be destroyed. Liu Du then sincerely led the people to dedicate themselves to the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara. Soon, something descended from the sky and circled around the pillar of the house where they resided. They were surprised and saw it was the Sutra of Avalokiteshvara. They had someone read it, and it was found that the widely recorded text did not contain this sentence. The ruler "未" was greatly pleased and thus spared the punishment. Thus, this city was spared from harm. (From the "Jewel Treasury" Volume 11, "Broad Record" Volume 110) Jin Dynasty Guo Xuanzhi, from Taiyuan, served as the Military Governor of Liangzhou in the fourth year of the Yixi era (408 AD). Yang Siping was accused of harming Fan Yuanzhi and others and thus subjected to the law, and Guo Xuanzhi was also imprisoned with him. He dedicated himself solely to the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara. One night, as he was about to sleep, he suddenly witnessed the light of the Bodhisattva illuminating the prison. He gazed upon, worshipped, and prayed, vowing for a long time until the light disappeared. Soon, Guo Xuanzhi was uniquely granted amnesty. After being released, he created images based on what he had seen and established a meditation hall. Later, he served in various places such as Lingling and Hengyang, and died in office. (From the "Jewel Treasury" Volume 17)

Jin Xinjiang's Yu Shaozhi, with the nickname Dao Fu, was the governor of Xiangdong during the Eastern Jin Dynasty. He had a close relationship with NanYang's Song Xie, who was his cousin and brother-in-law, and their affection was deep and enduring. Yu Shaozhi died in the first year of Yuanxing due to illness. In the first year of Yixi, he suddenly appeared in the form of a spirit to visit Song Xie. His appearance and attire were exactly as they had been in life, but his feet were bound with shackles. Upon arrival, he removed the shackles and placed them on the ground before sitting down. Song Xie asked, "Why have you come to see me?" Yu Shaozhi replied, "I have been temporarily allowed to return, and because of our close relationship, I have come to visit you." Song Xie asked about matters related to spirits and the supernatural, but Yu Shaozhi spoke vaguely and did not provide detailed answers. He only said, "You should strive for spiritual advancement, not kill living beings; if you cannot completely abstain, do not slaughter cows, and when eating meat, do not consume the heart." Song Xie asked, "Is the heart different from other parts of the body?" Yu Shaozhi replied, "The heart is the dwelling place of the benevolent spirit, and its sin is particularly heavy." He then inquired about Song Xie's relatives and discussed worldly affairs. At the end, he requested wine. Song Xie often drank wine with wormwood, so he prepared it for Yu Shaozhi. When the wine was served, Yu Shaozhi did not drink, saying there was a scent of wormwood. Song Xie said, "Is it because it is bad?" Yu Shaozhi replied, "All of us fear it, not just me." Yu Shaozhi had a loud and strong voice, and during this conversation, it was no different from his usual self. After a while, Song Xie's son Shuo Zhi came, and Yu Shaozhi heard the sound of his footsteps and became extremely frightened. He told Song Xie, "I am being oppressed by living energy and cannot stay here anymore; I will be separated from you for three years!" He then put on the shackles and stood up, and upon exiting the door, he disappeared. Song Xie later became a regular official, and indeed, he died three years later. (From *Jade Forest* 94 and *Record of the Marvelous* 324)

A Buddhist monk named Fa'an, a disciple of Master Yuan of Mount Lu, was traveling to Yangxin County during the end of the Yìxī era. At the time, tigers were rampant in the area, and there was a large sacred tree in the county with a temple built beneath it. The surrounding settlements housed hundreds of families. Every evening, a few people were killed by tigers. One day, Fa'an arrived in the county and stayed in a settlement. The people were afraid of the tigers and closed their doors early, and not knowing Fa'an, they did not accept him. Fa'an sat in meditation under the tree through the night. Early in the morning, a tiger carrying a person arrived and approached the tree. Seeing Fa'an, the tiger jumped around excitedly, lay down in front of him, and Fa'an taught it precepts. The tiger stood still and then left. By dawn, the people of the settlement followed the dead to the tree and saw Fa'an, greatly surprised. They thought he was a divine being, so the tiger did not harm them. From then on, the tiger problem ceased. The people became more respectful and amazed, and almost all of them followed the law (or religious precepts). Later, Fa'an wanted to paint an image on the mountain wall but could not find blue stone, so he wanted to use green stone, but there was no copper. He dreamed of someone passing by his bed and saying, "There are two bronze bells here, you can take them." In the morning, Fa'an dug them up and used them to complete the image. Later, when Master Yuan cast an image, Fa'an contributed one. The other one was borrowed by the governor of Wuchang, Xiong Wuhuan, to view and remained with him.

A Monk Named Zhi Tan During the Jin Dynasty, a monk named Zhi Tan, from the Qin Prefecture, was known for his true virtue and ascetic practices. He carried a begging bowl and a staff, providing for the four classes of people (monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen). He resided on Jiangshan Mountain. He often practiced the "Banzhou" meditation and was particularly skilled in divination and exorcism, with many of his predictions and rituals proving accurate. Emperor Yuanxian of the Jin Dynasty held him in high respect and venerated him. General Liu Yi, upon hearing of his rigorous practices, invited him to Gusu, where he was deeply loved and cherished. In the fifth year of the Yixi era (405 AD), a severe drought struck, causing ponds and lakes to dry up and crops to wither and die. Despite repeated prayers and sacrifices to mountains and rivers, there was no response for many days. General Yi then invited monks to perform a ritual meal, and Zhi Tan was among them. After the meal, he personally rode in a carriage, floating on rivers and streams. Literati, soldiers, and commoners from the entire state participated in the ritual. Zhi Tan, in the middle of the river, burned incense and performed worship with sincerity and enthusiasm. He then read aloud from the *Sutra of the Dragon King of the Sea*. As he began to read, clouds formed. Halfway through, rain fell in all directions. Just as he finished reading the scroll, heavy rain poured down, filling all ponds and fields. That year, there was a bountiful harvest. Liu Jingshu, who was serving as a Langzhongling under General Yi, personally attended this gathering and witnessed these events. This account is recorded in Chapter 63 of the *Jade Forest*.

During the Jin dynasty, Xiang Jing, courtesy name Fengren, was a man from Hedong. He had a daughter in Wuxing who died at a young age. The *Four-Character Notes* cite a story about a girl who died at a young age. When she first fell ill, she played with a small knife, and her mother took it away from her, causing the child to injure her mother's hand. A year after her death, the mother gave birth to another daughter, who was four years old. The girl asked her mother, "Where is the knife from before?" The mother replied, "It is not here." The girl said, "We argued over the knife before, so I hurt your hand. How can it not be here?" Her mother was greatly surprised and informed Xiang Jing about this. Xiang Jing asked, "Is the previous knife still around?" The mother replied, "I am too heartbroken to remember the past, so I did not keep it." Xiang Jing suggested, "Find several knives, put them together in one place, and let the girl choose." The girl saw them and was very happy, immediately picking up the original one and saying, "This is mine." The parents and others then realized that the deceased daughter had a clear memory of her past life. This story is cited in *Zhu Lin* volume 26 and *Guang Ji* volume 387, specifically up to the point where the girl takes the knife.

Zhaoshi Changhe was a man from Gaoyi in Zhao. At the age of nineteen, he fell ill for more than a month and died. Due to poverty, he could not be buried in time, and after four days, he revived. He recounted: "At the time of my initial death, I traveled southeast and saw two people repairing the road, fifty steps ahead of me. As I walked at varying speeds, the two people also adjusted their pace to match, always maintaining a distance of fifty steps. The path on either side was lined with dense thorns, all like eagle claws. Many people rushed into the thorns, suffering severe injuries, and blood flowed everywhere. Seeing me walking alone on a flat path, they all sighed and said, 'The Buddha's child walks alone on the great road.' As I continued, I came to a place with tile-roofed houses and colorful towers, numbering in the thousands. There was a particularly tall house, and on top of it, a man with a large and imposing appearance was sitting by the window, wearing a black robe with four seams. I bowed to him, and the man on the upper floor said, 'Is it you, Stone? We have been separated for more than two thousand years.' At that moment, I felt as if I remembered this separation. I knew a couple named Ma Mu Shengcheng and his wife, who had died many years ago. The man on the upper floor asked, 'Do you recognize Shengcheng?' I replied, 'I do.' He said, 'Shengcheng did not strive diligently in life and now serves as a cleaner for me; his wife, however, was diligent and lives very happily.'

He pointed to a room in the southwest and said, "Meng's wife is here." Meng's wife opened the window and saw Chang He, and gave heartfelt condolences, inquiring about the well-being of all members of his family. She said, "When Master Shi returns, you can visit again, and I will send a letter." Soon, she saw Meng the Chancellor carrying a basket, coming from the west of the hall, and also asked about the family news. The person on the veranda said, "Have you heard that Fish and Dragon have advanced spiritually? What have you practiced?" Chang He replied, "I do not eat fish or meat, do not drink alcohol, always recite the scriptures, and help those in pain." The person on the veranda said, "What has been said is not false." After talking for a while, the person on the veranda asked the chief recorder, "Check the list of Master Shi, do not make mistakes." The chief recorder checked the list and said, "I have thirty years of life left." The person on the veranda said, "Do you want to return?" Chang He replied, "I wish to return." Then, he ordered the chief recorder to send two officials by carriage to escort him. Chang He bowed and took leave, then got on the carriage and returned. On the way, there were more relay stations with officials and civilians storing food and supplies. Suddenly, he arrived home, disliked the smell of the corpse, did not want to touch it, and stood at the head of the corpse. He saw his deceased sister-in-law push him from behind, causing him to fall on the face of the corpse, and thus he revived. Monk Zhi Fashan, who had not yet entered the monastic life, heard what He said and thus decided to enter the path of the Dharma. Fashan was a person of the Xianhe period. Jewel Forest, Volume Seven, General Records, Chapter Three Hundred and Eighty-Three.

A Buddhist monk named Shan, sometimes written as Shan, with the courtesy name Dao Kai, whose origin is unknown. In a separate biography in the *High Monks' Transmission* and the story of Buddha adjusting his boat, it is said that he was from Dunhuang and originally surnamed Meng. He became a monk at a young age and wanted to live in remote mountains and valleys, so he first stopped eating grains. Initially, he took some unspecified substance, and three years later, he began to take pine resin. Thirty years later, he only occasionally swallowed small stones. After the stones passed, he stopped drinking alcohol and eating meat and fruits. His body fears cold and wind, and he only eats pepper and ginger. His strength is weak, but his skin is smooth and lustrous, and he walks like a flying bird. Mountain gods frequently tested him, but he never wavered. Immortals often came to him, but he also did not tolerate them. He would chew garlic to repel them. He sat quietly and meditated, never sleeping day or night. He stayed for a long time in Bao Han. In the second year of Jianwu reign of Shi Hu, he was brought from Xiping and arrived at Ye. He did not ride in boats or carriages and traveled over seven hundred miles a day.

A Man of Asceticism and Benevolence Passing through Nan'an, he initiated a young boy, aged thirteen or fourteen, as a novice monk. The boy's behavior was also mature. Upon arrival, he resided in the Zhaode Pagoda, wearing coarse and tattered robes, often exposing his back. Inside the house, he built a small pavilion, about eight or nine feet high, and wove reeds into a canopy, meditating within it. He abstained from food for seven years, regularly taking various medicines, which had the scent of pine resin and poria. Skilled in treating eye diseases, he frequently traveled through the countryside, healing the common people. Both local and distant nobles offered gifts, which he accepted and distributed, leaving nothing for himself. At the end of Shi Hu's reign, foreseeing the chaos, he moved south to Xuchang with his disciples. In the third year of Shengping, he came to Jianye, then traveled to Panyu, residing on Luofu Mountain, resting in the shade of the forest, finding joy in solitude. He died in July of that year, leaving a final instruction to be buried in the woods, which his disciples followed. In the first year of Xingning, Chen Jun's Yuan Yanbo, as the governor of Nanhai, climbed this mountain with his brother Ying Shu to pay respects to his remains, burning incense and performing rituals. *Jewel Forest, Chapter 27.*

In the Qin state, there was a man named Xu Yi from Gaolü. When he was young, he served the law and became a high official under Fu Jian. During the later years of Fu Jian's reign, widespread military uprisings broke out, and Xu Yi was captured by the rebels, who intended to kill him. They buried his feet and tied his hair to a tree. At night, Xu Yi devoted himself to chanting the name of Guanyin (Avalokiteshvara). After a while, he fell asleep and had a dream where a person told him, "The situation is urgent, why should you sleep?" Xu Yi woke up suddenly and saw that the guards were all tired and asleep. He then tried to move himself, and his hands were freed, and his feet also escaped. Thus, he fled. After a hundred steps, he hid in a small patch of grass and heard the pursuers passing by, their torches arranged in a star-like formation, circling around the patch of grass, but they never saw him. When dawn came, the rebels dispersed, and Xu Yi returned to the temple in Ye, thus escaping. This story is recorded in *Zhulin* (Jade Forest) Volume 17, Chapter 110. In the Qin state, there was another man named Bi Luan from Dongping. When he was young, he served the law and followed Murong Chui on a northern campaign, where he was captured by the enemy and fled alone on horseback. The enemy's cavalry was about to catch up, so Bi Luan devoted himself to chanting the name of Guanyin. After escaping, he entered a deep mountain and lost his way. He then focused his mind on returning, and in the middle of the night, he saw a monk in monastic robes holding a staff, who showed him the way. Thus, he found his way back and arrived safely home. This story is also recorded in *Zhulin* (Jade Forest) Volume 17, Chapter 110. In the Song dynasty, there was a monk named Fa Chen. On his deathbed, he said, "A man from Songshan told me, 'The General Liu in Jiangdong should receive the mandate of heaven.' He also gave me thirty-two jade disks and a piece of gold as proof." Emperor Song heard this and ordered the monk Hu Yi to go to Songshan, where he practiced for seven days and seven nights. Hu Yi dreamed of an old man with a long beard who pointed the way. When he woke up, he clearly remembered the location and dug it up. This story is recorded in *Guangji* (Extensive Records) Volume 276.

The Translator of Merit: Master Kao Na This is called the Translator of Merit. According to the *Biographies of Eminent Monks*, it means "merit armor," but the record is in error. He was a prince of Kāpiśa (Kāśmīra) who, at a young age, entered the monastic life and was known as a Tripiṭaka Master. In the early Song dynasty, he came to China, where he translated many important Buddhist scriptures. His observance of precepts was extremely high, unmatched by others. Master Huiguan, a monk, admired his virtue and invited him to the capital, where he lived at the Jetavana Monastery. At that time, those who came to see him suspected that he was not an ordinary person, but his spiritual essence was profound and mysterious, beyond comprehension. He once attended a gathering at Dinglin Temple on Zhongshan Mountain. At that time, many laypeople and monks picked various flowers and placed them under the monk's seat to test for a true sage. The flowers under the other monks' seats withered, but the flowers under Master Kao's seat remained fresh and vibrant. Thus, the capital city's respect for him increased. He passed away on the 18th day of the 9th month in the 8th year of the Yuanjia era (431 AD). He died without any illness, just sitting in the full lotus position, with his sleeves folded and hands together, for two nights, with his appearance unchanged. At that time, some thought he was in deep meditation, but later a note was found under the seat saying he had attained the second level of arhatship, thus it was known that he had passed away. His disciples who were by his side smelled a fragrant smoke. Over 200 people from the capital came to the gathering, and that evening, as they chanted scriptures, people gathered outside the door, filling the steps. As dawn approached, a dense cloud appeared in the southwest, and soon a creature, about a foot long, circled his body and then disappeared. All present saw this. Before he passed away, he composed thirty verses and said to his disciples, "You can show these to monks from India." This account is found in Chapter 42 of *The Jewel Forest of the Dharma Realm*.

A Man Named Chen Anju from Xiangyang County Chen Anju was a native of Xiangyang County in the state of Song. His elder uncle was involved in shamanistic customs, performing dances and sacrifices, with temple images and structures filling his house. His father, on the other hand, alone revered and trusted Buddhist law, practicing daily fasting and purification. After the elder uncle died without an heir, the father designated Anju as his successor. Although Anju inherited the elder uncle's house, he pursued a more refined and disciplined life, abandoning excessive feasting and sacrificial rituals. As a result, he fell ill severely, and during his episodes, he would sing songs to the gods, becoming confused and dazed. This persisted for a year, and his resolve grew stronger. He often vowed, "If my determination to kill (possibly referring to himself) were to be thwarted, I would first cut off my limbs before proceeding." His family tried to dissuade him, but Anju refused to listen. After two years, in the first year of Yongchu, his illness flared up, and he seemingly died, but with warmth still in his chest, so the family did not bury him. On the seventh night, those watching noticed the corpse's feet moving as if wind was blowing, clothes and bedding fluttering, and then Anju made a sound of revival. Initially, the family feared the corpse was coming back to life and fled, but later, he began to move slightly and eventually asked for water. The family was delighted and asked where he had come from. Anju then detailed what he had experienced: initially, there was someone resembling a messenger; carrying many knives, he called for Anju to follow. The followers wanted to bind him, but the messenger said, "This person has good fortune, do not bind him."

Traveled about three hundred li, Anju arrived at a city or administrative center where the buildings were very well-organized. The messenger went to several places, including the residence of the bureau director. Finally, someone gave paper and ink to Anju and said, "You can write down twenty-four names of the dead." Anju wrote down the names as instructed. A servant then came out and shouted loudly, "Anju, you may enter." Upon entering, it was said there was an order to hand over to the prison for investigating crimes. Two officials debated, one saying, "Use the large chain," and the other, "This person has quite a bit of fortune; a three-foot chain will suffice." Unable to reach a decision, they all looked at the documents for a long time and eventually decided to give him a three-foot chain. After a while, a noble person appeared with several attendants, his appearance very elegant. He asked Anju, "How did you come here?" Anju explained his situation. The noble person said, "Your uncle has a crime, but he should be recorded and punished. Because of a small amount of previous good fortune, you temporarily got to wander around, so you dared to report. I and your father had old acquaintances when young. Seeing you still, I can take you on a tour." The prison guard did not release the chain, saying, "The lord has no instruction; I dare not act on my own." The noble person replied, "Just give it to me; I will not let him escape." So they released him. The noble person took Anju to all the hells, showing him all the sufferings, which roughly matched the scriptures. While touring, a messenger came and said, "The lord calls Anju." Feeling confused and fearful, Anju sought help from the noble person. The noble person reassured him, "You yourself have no crime; just answer truthfully, and there will be no worry."

At the assembly, there were hundreds of shackled prisoners who were brought forward all at once, and "Anju" was in the third position. Once they reached the steps below, a man wearing a crown stood before the prisoners and read the records of their crimes. The first entry stated that when the man married, he and his wife took an oath: whether they had children or not, they would never abandon each other. However, the man was originally a high-ranking official in a religious or scholarly organization, and his wife also followed the same path. Together, they led and educated many disciples, but he committed adultery and abandoned his original wife. The wife frequently complained of her injustice. The judge said, "You both violated the great principle of your vow, and you are not guilty of two crimes, but one; the relationship between teacher and disciple is sacred, and committing adultery is akin to incest, which is a grave sin. Hand this case over to the legal department for sentencing!" Next, the judge read the petition of the second woman. Her name is forgotten, but she said her home was in Huangshui Village, Junxian County, Nanyang. She had placed cooking utensils at the mouth of a special stove. One day, the woman slept heavily, and her baby crawled on the stove, defecating in the cooking utensils. Upon waking, the woman immediately apologized to the gods and washed the utensils thoroughly. However, her brother-in-law scolded her, saying there were no gods or spirits, and that she allowed such filth to occur. The officials heard about this and took her to be punished. The judge said, "Sleeping heavily is not a fault, and the baby was innocent. The woman had already apologized to the gods, so she is not guilty; your brother-in-law's scolding is unrighteous, and he is spreading false accusations against the spirits. He can be brought in for punishment." Shortly after, he was brought in with a red rope around his neck.

Anju, the person at the lower level, read the list of names, and was complained about by his elder brother. The lord said, "This person worships the Buddha, a great virtuous person! His elder brother killed the innocent and slandered the people, and should be thoroughly investigated; because he had a little good fortune in the past, he was not punished. The elder brother now again defames the innocent?" The instruction was given to take him in, but before they could arrive, the lord sent Anju back, saying, "If you can return, good deeds will be accomplished, you can live to be ninety-three years old, strive hard! Do not come again." Anju left and went to the hall, the official said, "You can withdraw your name from the list of the dead." Thus, Anju removed his name one by one, and then decided to visit the noble person. The noble person also arrived, saying, "Knowing you have no other intentions, it is very good that you can return, strive hard to cultivate virtue; my own virtue is slight, I cannot ascend to heaven to receive reward, here to assist the lord, also enjoy the beauty of leisurely and wealthy spiritual life. My home is in Wan, my surname is [surname] and my name is [name], when you return, convey my greetings: deeply adhere to the law, do not violate the Buddhist precepts, you can fully tell them what you have seen."
A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

During the Southern Song Dynasty, there was a monk named Senggui from Wudang Temple. At that time, Zhang Yu from Jingzhao often invited Senggui to his home for support. In the first year of Yongchou, on the fifth day of the twelfth month, Senggui died suddenly without any illness, but he revived after two days. He recounted what happened: On the fifth night of the fifth watch, he heard a clamor in the street. Soon after, he saw five people carrying torches and holding banners, coming straight into the house. They shouted at Senggui, causing him to fall into a trance-like state, and the five people then bound him with a red rope and took him away. They led him to a mountain with no grass or trees, the soil was hard and black, like stone or iron. On both sides of the mountain, there were piles of white bones, stretching for dozens of miles. They reached a three-way fork where there was a very tall and strong man, wearing armor and holding weapons. He asked the five people, "How many of you have come?" They replied, "Only one person!" The five then took Senggui into one of the paths, and soon they arrived at the outskirts of a city. There were dozens of houses, built from earth, with a standing tree in front of the house, over ten zhang tall. On top of the tree was an iron beam shaped like a seesaw, with bins on either side, storing earth. The earth had different sizes, some as large as ten dou, others as small as five dou.

A man appeared, dressed entirely in red, and spoke to the monk Guī: "When you were born, what merits and faults did you have? Speak the truth, do not speak falsely." Guī was in great fear and unable to answer. The red-clothed person, acting like an official, said, "Can we open the ledger to check their merits and faults?" After a while, an official arrived at the long wooden stand, lifted a small chest of earth, and hung it on an iron beam to weigh it. As if sensing the imbalance, the official told Guī, "This is the scale for measuring merits and faults. Your merits are few and your faults many; you should be punished first." Soon, a man dressed in formal attire approached and said to Guī, "You are a monk, why don't you recite Buddha's name? I have heard that repentance can help cross the eight difficulties." Guī then sincerely recited Buddha's name. The formally dressed man told the official, "Can you weigh this person again? Since he is a disciple of Buddha, it is fortunate that he can be saved." The official then reweighed the chest, and the scale became perfectly balanced. They then took Guī to the supervisor's presence to debate the matter. The supervisor held a brush and looked at the ledger, hesitating for a long time. Another man, dressed in red with a black hat, wearing seals and holding a jade tablet, came and said, "There is no name of this person on the ledger." The supervisor was startled and ordered his attendants to take Guī away. Soon, five men appeared with their hands tied behind their backs. The supervisor said, "Kill the ghosts, why did you bring people indiscriminately? They were whipped." Shortly after, a messenger announced, "The Heavenly Emperor calls the Daoist priest." Upon arriving at the Heavenly Emperor's palace, Guī saw the journey and experiences, all adorned with golden treasures and brilliant light, dazzling to the point of not being able to look directly.

The emperor, adorned with red robes and a jeweled crown, surrounded by precious treasures, said to the monk, "You are a monk, why are you not diligently practicing your monastic duties but instead being captured as a demon?" The monk bowed to the Buddhas, praying for blessings. The emperor then said, "Your life is not yet over, and you will be reborn. You should diligently strive and not frequently visit the homes of laypeople! Killing demons and taking people is often unjust and excessive, like you." The monk asked, "What can I do to avoid such misfortune?" The emperor replied, "Widely establishing good deeds is the best; if you cannot, you can observe the eight-precept observance. This will help you avoid misfortune in life and be reborn in heaven after death, which is also good." After speaking, the emperor sent the monk away. Shortly after, the monk saw a monastery with many monks, including Master Bai, the head of the Wudang Temple, and his disciple Huixin. The monastery was grand and orderly, and the monk requested to stay there. One of the monks said, "This is a blessed place, not suitable for you to stay." The messenger then took the monk back to the house of Yu.

He Danzhi, a man from Donghai, served as the Grand Agricultural Minister during the Song dynasty. He did not believe in religious teachings and often engaged in cruel and harmful acts. During the Yongchun era, he fell ill and saw a ghost. The ghost had a very tall and strong form, with a bull's head and a human body, holding an iron tool, and guarded him day and night. Feeling distressed and restless, he had Taoists create talismans and inscriptions to ward off evil, but still saw the ghost as before. A monk named Huiyi, whom he knew, heard of his illness and visited him. Danzhi told him about what he had seen. Huiyi said, "This is the 'Niu Tou A Pang' ghost." He explained that retribution for sins and blessings is not obscured, and it is only what one has brought upon oneself. "If you can change your heart and turn to the law (or religion), then this ghost will naturally disappear." Danzhi remained stubborn and unrepentant, and soon after, he died. This story is recorded in the "Zhu Lin" (Jewel Forest), entry 83. Monk Zhuhui Chi, a native of Xinye, lived in the Four-Tiered Temple in Jiangling. In the second year of the Yongchun era, he died. His disciples set up a seven-day memorial service. On that day, as evening approached and after burning incense, a monk named Daoxian went to visit Zhuhui Chi's disciples. Arriving at the front of the room, he suddenly saw a figure that appeared human. Looking closely, it was Zhuhui Chi. His appearance and clothing were no different from when he was alive. He said to Daoxian, "Did you enjoy the meat you ate this morning?" Daoxian replied, "Yes, it was enjoyable." Zhuhui Chi said, "I am suffering in the hell of hungry dogs because I ate meat." Daoxian was terrified and did not get a chance to answer. Zhuhui Chi spoke again, "If you don't believe me, look at my back." He turned his back to show Daoxian, who saw three yellow dogs, half the shape of donkeys, with very red eyes that illuminated the room. They seemed about to bite Zhuhui Chi but then stopped. Daoxian was terrified and fainted. After a long time, he regained consciousness and told the whole story. This account is recorded in the "Zhu Lin" (Jewel Forest), entry 94.

Jin Wang Lian, courtesy name Xuanming, was from Langya and served as a Shizhong in the Song dynasty. His father, Wen, courtesy name Jiyan, was a Zhongshu Ling in the Jin dynasty. Wen had an acquaintance, a Vajra monk, who greatly admired Wen's bearing and often told his fellow monks, "If I were to be reborn as a son of this person, it would be enough for my near wishes." Wen, upon hearing this, playfully responded, "The monk's virtue and talent, just suitable to be a disciple's child!" Soon after, the monk fell ill and passed away. A year or so after his death, Jin Wang Lian was born. From the moment he could speak, he could understand foreign languages and recognize exotic treasures, rare vessels, and precious jewels. He could name things he had never seen and knew their place of origin. He naturally loved all the monks more than Han people. Everyone believed that the monk knew his previous life, so Wen named him Alian, and thus it became a great name. Zhu Lin 26, Guang Ji 387, Bian Zheng Lun 8 Chen Ziliang annotation, citing the Míng Xiáng Jì (Record of Auspicious Signs): Wang Wen of Langya had no children and often prayed to Guanyin for a child. One day, while traveling, he met a Hu Seng, whom he greatly admired. The Hu Seng said, "After my death, I will be reborn as your son." Soon after, the monk died, and three months later, Wang Wen's wife became pregnant. When the child was born, he could speak and understood the languages of the sixteen kingdoms of the Western Regions. He was very intelligent and had a dignified bearing, which was later identified as that of Wang Yuanning, a Shangshu in the Jin dynasty. Therefore, he was given the nickname Alian, and the events of his previous life were verified.

In the *Zhu Lin* of the Song dynasty, which cites the *Jin* (Treatise) and follows the *Guang Ji* (Broad Records), there is a story about Sun Daode, a man from Yizhou who served as a Taoist priest. He was over fifty years old and had no children. Living near a monastery, during the reign of Emperor Wen of Liu Song (424-427 AD), a Buddhist monk told him, "If you truly wish to have a son, you should devote yourself wholeheartedly to the worship and recitation of the Sutra of the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara. This is something you can hope for." Sun Daode then ceased his Taoist practices and devoted his heart in sincere faith to Avalokiteshvara. In a short period of time, he had a dream response, and his wife became pregnant, eventually giving birth to a son. This story is recorded in *Fa Yuan Zhu Lin* (Treasury of the Dharma Jewel) and *Tai Ping Guang Ji* (Broad Records of the Great Peace). Another story in *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest) concerns a monk named Qing from the Song and Qi dynasties, who was from Jiangling. His family adhered to the law, and when he was around ten years old, a fortune-teller predicted, "Your lifespan will not exceed thirty-six years." His parents, brothers, and he himself were greatly alarmed. Qing increased his diligence in reverence, fasting, and strict discipline. At the age of seventeen, towards the end of the reign of Emperor Wen of Liu Song, he fell gravely ill. His family intensified their prayers and offerings, and even engaged in superstitious practices to seek blessings, but his illness did not improve. At this time, a female witch said, "This young man has powerful good fortune and virtue, which even demons and evil spirits cannot approach; he has the protection of good spirits. However, his illness has lasted a long time, and his fate or destiny may be limited. There is a method to divining one's fate. I have served the heavenly deities and understand this technique. I will try it for you." She set up an offering of wine and meat in the wild, burned money, and performed rituals over seven days and seven nights during the seventh day of the seventh month. She said, "I have now seen a response. I saw various good spirits praying for this young man, and he has been granted two additional years of life. His illness will certainly be cured, so there is nothing to worry about." Qing then recovered, and his devotion became even more meticulous. He lived for twenty-four more years after this, as the witch had predicted, with each calculation representing twelve years. This story is recorded in *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest).

The Crown Prince of Wei, a native of Liang Commandery, diligently followed the law, and his sons also adhered to it. However, his wife was confused and closed-minded, and she did not believe in the Buddhist teachings. In the early years of the Yüanjia era, their daughter, who was fourteen years old, died of illness. Seven days later, she revived and said that a high seat and the *Infinite Life Sutra* could be placed there. The Crown Prince immediately arranged for the sutra and the seat. Although the daughter had previously fasted, abstained, and prostrated, she had never read the sutra before. Now, with the addition of the characters "广记引" (from the *Zhu Lin* and *Taiping Guangji*), she ascended the seat and recited the sutra fluently. She then spoke to her father, saying, "When I died, I went to the Land of Infinite Life and saw my father, elder brother, and myself. There were already large lotus flowers in the pond, and I will be reborn there later. However, my mother was not there, and I could not bear this suffering. I was determined, so I returned to report." Her words ended, and she died again. Her mother then began to respect and believe in the teachings.

Cao Xing of the Song dynasty was a native of Xingning and had a strong belief in Buddhism. He once followed the monk Seng Rong (Tanyi) and received the Eight Precepts at that time. Cao Xing was often led by robbers, and his husband managed to escape, but his wife was imprisoned and beaten for several days. At one point, the county experienced a fire, and the prisoners were released to the side of the road. Coincidentally, Seng Rong happened to be walking with them and passed by the prisoners. The wife shouted in surprise, "Reverend, how can you save me?" Seng Rong replied, "I am weak in spirit and cannot help you. You should diligently recite the name of Guanyin, perhaps you may be spared." The wife then began to recite the name of Guanyin day and night. After about ten days, she had a dream in which a monk stepped on her feet and said, "Hurry up and get up!" The wife woke up in shock, and suddenly all her shackles and fetters were undone. She ran towards the door, but it was still closed, and the guards were very strict. There was no way to escape, and she worried that someone might wake up. So, she put the shackles back on and soon fell asleep again. In another dream, the same monk told her, "The door is open now!" The wife woke up and ran out, finding the guards all asleep. She walked calmly away. It was very dark at night, and she walked for several miles before suddenly encountering someone. The wife was afraid and fell to the ground, but when she looked up, it was her husband. They supported each other in joy and sorrow, and at night, they went to Seng Rong. Seng Rong hid them, and they thus escaped. This was at the beginning of the Yuanjia era. The story is recorded in Volume 17 of the "Book of Jewels" and Volume 110 of the "Comprehensive Records."

In the early years of the Yuanjia period of the Song dynasty, there was a novice monk named Tan Wujie, known as Huanglong, who chanted the *Guanshiyin Sutra* and engaged in pure cultivation and austere practices. Together with his disciples, fifty-two in number, he went to seek the land of the Buddha. They endured all kinds of dangers and hardships, yet his resolve became even stronger. After arriving at Shāwèi in Tianzhu (India), he encountered a group of wild elephants on the road. Monk Tan Wujie offered the sutra, chanted the name, and devoted himself to the Buddha. A lion emerged from the forest, and the elephants were frightened and fled. Later, a herd of wild cattle, roaring, came to attack. Monk Tan Wujie again recited the name and dedicated himself to the Buddha as before. A large vulture flew over, and the cattle were frightened and scattered. Thus, he was able to escape unharmed. (Chapter 75 of the *Fa Yuan Zhu Lin*)

During the Tang dynasty, there was a man named Wen Bo from the region of Dangyu in the East Sea. His younger brother enjoyed gambling and eventually squandered all their family wealth. There was a temple within a military camp, and sometimes people passing by would offer money to the Buddha. The brother frequently stole these offerings. After a long time, he fell ill with leprosy. A diviner said, "The illness is caused by stealing the offerings to the Buddha." The father became angry and said, "What kind of god is the Buddha, causing my son to fall ill? I will try to steal the offerings myself. If I too fall ill, then the diviner's words are true." He stole four brocade belts that the wife of a former county magistrate named He Xin had offered as a canopy and used them as a belt. Within less than a hundred days, the father also fell ill, and the illness began at the place where the belt was worn.
