This event took place during the early years of the reign of Emperor Yuan of Liu Song. It is recorded in the *Zhu Lin* (Pearl Forest) and *Guang Ji* (Comprehensive Records). In the Liu Song dynasty, there was a Buddhist monk named Dao Jiong from the county of Haozhi in the region of Fufeng. Originally, he had the surname Ma and had pure and refined learning, being well-known for his scholarly achievements at a young age. In the ninth month of the second year of the reign of Emperor Yuan, he was in Luoyang, the capital city, conducting a ritual dedicated to the Bodhisattva Samantabhadra for someone. About forty people, including both monks and laypeople, had already been participating in the ritual for seven days. On the day of the midday meal, a person suddenly appeared, wearing trousers and a jacket and riding a horse, entering the hall and dismounting to pay respects to the Buddha. Dao Jiong considered the person to be an ordinary person and did not give him special attention. The person then mounted his horse, waved his whip, and suddenly disappeared. A bright red light spread across the sky, lasting for a long time before fading away. Three years later, in the twelfth month, Dao Jiong was again in a layperson's home, conducting a ritual dedicated to the Bodhisattva Samantabhadra. On the day the ritual was about to end, two monks appeared, dressed in ordinary clothing, and came directly to pay respects to the Buddha. Dao Jiong thought they were common monks and did not show much respect. He casually asked where they lived, and they replied, "We live in a previous garrison."

At that time, among the common people, there was a man named Zhang Dao. He felt that the monk was unusual and worshiped him with utmost sincerity. The monk went out and walked about ten steps. Suddenly, flying dust shot straight up to the sky. Looking back at the monk, one could no longer see where he had gone. Jiong came to the capital with his fellow students after seven years. At that time, the Grand Works Commissioner He Shangzhi had just begun to construct the Nanjian Monastery, and Jiong stayed there. At night, he suddenly saw four people in a new carriage, accompanied by four attendants. They came into the house and called Jiong to ride with them. Jiong was surprised that they came at night and hesitated to speak. He closed his eyes and did not feel himself being lifted onto the carriage. Soon, they arrived at the Sheng Bridge behind the county. There, he saw a noble person wearing a robe made of fine cloth, sitting on a bed with an umbrella-like canopy, its shape resembling a canopy. There were hundreds of attendants and guards, all wearing yellow clothes. Seeing Jiong, the noble person was surprised and said, "The traveler on the *Ban Zhou* path, with sincere heart and far-reaching aspirations, wants to know the place. Why bring him here?" He immediately sent people to escort Jiong back. When they reached the monastery gate, the people who had escorted him were gone, and the gate was still closed. After knocking and calling for a long time, the monks inside the monastery were all startled and reported it. They opened the door and let him in. Looking at the room where he had stayed, it was still locked. Zhu Lin, Volume 17.

Lǐ Dàn, courtesy name Shìzé, was a man from Guangling, renowned in his village for his filial piety, diligence, and simplicity. In the third year of the Yuán Jiā era, on the fourteenth day of the first month, he suddenly fell ill, but his heart remained warm, and he revived after seven days, being fed porridge. One day, a person carrying a banner of faith came to his bedside, saying that the lord of the prefecture had called for him. Lǐ Dàn immediately followed the messenger. They traveled directly north, the road being very flat and clean. Upon arrival, they saw city gates and towers that were tall and beautiful, resembling the palaces of today. A messenger was sent to comfort and console him, and he was called to come forward to the main hall. There, he saw thirty people, all wearing plain clothes and blue headbands, sitting in an orderly and solemn manner. One person sat on the east side, wearing a robe and hiding in the mundane, with about a hundred attendants on either side. The person looked at Lǐ Dàn and spoke to the seated people, saying, "We will show you the various prisons, to make the world know."

Dawn heard the words and raised his head to look all around, completely losing the previous place, realizing he was in the underworld. He saw a crowd of sinners suffering various punishments, groaning and shouting, a sight that was unbearable to look at. Soon, a messenger appeared and said, "The Lord of the Mansion believes in you; you can return and go, and we will come to greet you again." Therefore, he returned. In the sixth year of January, he died again, but seven days later, he came back to life. He described what he saw, which was similar in detail to before. Some of the sins were reported by a messenger to the family, saying that when he was born, he committed a sin and was ordered to perform good deeds. The messenger called out names and surnames, and familiar faces and fellow villagers were found by Dawn following the words. He also said, "In the year Jia Shen, a plague will spread, killing all evil people. Buddhist disciples, by performing the Eight Precepts and practicing good deeds with the heart, can be exempted." Dawn originally practiced Daoism as a priest, but he wanted to abandon the Daoist scriptures due to the advice and control of Daoist followers, so he engaged in both practices and often preached, performing the Eight Precepts. The Sixth Chapter of the Treasure Forest of Dharma.

During the reign of Emperor Wu of the Liu Song dynasty, Zheng Xianzhi, a high-ranking official and servant to the emperor, traveled with the imperial procession to the capital city in the fourth year of the Yuanjia era. In the evening, he died suddenly. His spirit then spoke to someone, saying, "My life span has long ended, and I should have passed away long ago. However, due to the recent years of respecting and believing in Buddhism, releasing animals, and making charitable donations, by this merit, my life was extended by several years. Retribution in the visible and invisible worlds is as if it were an echo or shadow. One should abandon worldly affairs and uphold the great teaching." At that time, many noble and wealthy people heard of this. (Zhu Lin, Volume 6) Zhou Zong, a person from Feiru, Guangling, followed General Liu Yan to the northern campaign in the seventh year of the Yuanjia era. The imperial army suffered defeat, and Zhou Zong fled with six fellow townspeople. Wandering north of Pengcheng, they encountered an empty temple with no monks or disciples. Inside the temple, there were statues made of crystal. They collectively stole the statues and sold them at the military camp. One of them was weak and ill, and the others looked down on him, so he did not get a share. After each returned home, within three to four years, Zhou Zong and four others died one after another from leprosy, while the one who did not get a share remained unharmed. (Zhu Lin, Volume 79; Guangji, Volume 116)
A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Shao of Liu Song, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

Wang Wenxuan, a person from the Song dynasty, was from Hebei and believed in Buddhism quite a lot. In the ninth year of the reign of Emperor Wu of Liu Song, his mother passed away, and his brother died. On the night of the full moon, in the morning, he suddenly saw his brother's body on the altar, which was no different from usual. He was lost and sighed, asking for food and drink. Wang Wenxuan then tried to speak to the ghost, saying, "If you did good deeds in life, according to the scriptures, you should have been reborn in heaven. If you were reborn in the human world, why did you become a ghost?" The ghost hesitated and looked around, remaining silent without answering. That night, Wang Wenxuan dreamed of his brother, who said, "The good deeds done in life have been rewarded with rebirth in heaven. The ghost on the altar in the morning is just a demon, not my body. I am afraid that you will doubt and be surprised, so I came to tell you." The next day, Wang Wenxuan invited a monk to recite the *Shou Leng Yan Jing* (Sutra of the Buddha's Protection) and had someone tie the ghost up. The ghost then fled under the bed and out the door, and its appearance became slightly ugly and evil. The whole family was terrified and scolded and drove it away. The ghost said, "I'm hungry and asking for food!" After several days, it left. Soon, there was a red-skinned, tall, and strong ghost at the mother's altar. Wang Wenxuan's eldest son, Xiao Zu, spoke with it, and they conversed back and forth thoroughly. At first, they were afraid, but later they became accustomed to it. The ghost also began to attach itself and become familiar, living and moving around as if it were part of the family. At that time, the capital city spread the news, and people came and went, and the streets were crowded. At the time, there were monks from Nanlin Temple and Lingwei Temple who spoke with the ghost, and they also spoke very kindly and politely.

The ghost said: "In a past life, I was once noble and dignified, but I committed many evil deeds, and the retribution has not been completed, resulting in this ghostly form. In the year of the Tiger, there were four hundred ghosts who spread great epidemics, causing disasters for those who deserved them, not the wicked or those who do not follow the path; I committed many violent acts, often bestowed excessive blessings, thus I came to oversee this." The monk offered food to the ghost, who said, "I have my own food and cannot accept this." The monk asked, "Do ghosts know where I came from and why I became a monk?" The ghost replied, "I came from among humans to become a monk, due to the karmic connection and original vow." The monk asked about the fate of the living and the dead, and the ghost answered in a general way. There were many miraculous occurrences; the details were numerous and detailed, thus not recorded in full. The monk said, "Humans and ghosts have different paths; since you do not seek food, why stay for a long time?" The ghost said, "There is a woman here who should be captured, but she follows the precepts diligently, thus difficult to capture. Recently, I have been delayed due to this reason. Causing trouble for the host, I feel considerable shame." From this point onward, the ghost was not often seen, and those who came to visit later only heard words. It was the tenth year of the Yuanjia era. On March 28th, the ghost said, "I am temporarily staying, and you have spent your entire family's resources to create merit, are so fearful, how can I stay for a long time?" The monk said, "Allow you to stay temporarily, why occupy the spirit table of the deceased?" The ghost replied, "Those who have passed away in your family, each has their own destination; this seat is empty, thus temporarily occupying it." Thus, the ghost departed. This is from the sixth chapter of the *Zhu Lin*.

During the Song Dynasty, a monk named Tan Yuan, whose name is also recorded as He Tan Yuan in the *Guang Ji* (Volume 114), was from Lujiang. His father, Wan Shou, held the title of *Yushi Zhongcheng* (a high-ranking official). Tan Yuan observed the Dharma with great dedication and upheld the Bodhisattva precepts. At the age of eighteen, in the ninth year of the Yuanjia era, he experienced the death of his father, which led to deep grief and illness, nearly causing him to lose his life. Beyond crying and wailing, he dedicated himself to the Pure Land, hoping for a response or manifestation. Tan Yuan regularly invited several monks, including *Seng Han*, to his home. He frequently confessed his past sins to *Seng Han*, fearing that there might be obstacles, but eventually there was no response. *Seng Han* often encouraged him, urging him not to become complacent. On the sixteenth day of the second month of the tenth year, during the fourth watch of the night, after completing the recitation of scriptures, the other monks had already fallen asleep. Suddenly, Tan Yuan began to sing and chant on his own. *Seng Han* was startled and asked him what had happened. Tan Yuan replied, "I saw the Buddha's body, golden in color, with a shape and size like the image of a Buddha statue. His body was surrounded by golden light, with floating flames several feet high. Banners and flowers surrounded him, filling the void with their beauty and wonder, beyond description." At the time, Tan Yuan was in the western hall, where he said the Buddha came from the west, turned around to face the west, stood facing the central hall, and called for him to leave quickly. Tan Yuan, who was usually weak and breathless, showed signs of life that night, appearing vigorous and joyful. His face lit up with pleasure, and he immediately washed his hands. *Seng Han* placed incense in his hand and took flowers from the garden, scattering them towards the Buddha. His mother said to Tan Yuan, "If you are leaving now, do you not miss me?" Tan Yuan said nothing and soon after lay down suddenly. The family, who had long been believers, heard of this miraculous event and were all joyful and respectful, not overly sad or fearful. Tan Yuan passed away at the fifth watch of the night, and the house was filled with a fragrant aroma that lasted for several days. This event is recorded in *Zhu Lin* (Volume 15) and *Guang Ji* (Volume 114).

Shi Zhitong, a nun from the Song dynasty, was a simple and tranquil nun from the capital city. She was young and beautiful, but not firmly committed to her faith. In the ninth year of the reign of Emperor Wu of Liu Song (432 AD), after the death of her teacher, she left the monastic life and married a man named Liang Qunfu from Wei Prefecture. They had a son who was seven years old. The family was very poor and lacked clothing. Shi Zhitong had several scrolls of the *Amitabha Sutra* and *Lotus Sutra*. She washed and pounded these scrolls to make clothes for her son. After a year, the child fell ill, experiencing confusion, fear, and a condition where his body peeled off like a burn, with small white worms emerging. He suffered from severe pain and fever, crying day and night. He often heard voices in the air saying, "Destroying the sutras to make clothes, you receive this severe retribution." The child died after ten days.

Two daughters of the Song Lun family, residents of Dongguan Zengcheng, were cousins at the time. In the ninth year of the Yanchang era (432 AD), the elder sister was ten years old, and the younger sister was nine. Living in a remote village, they were ignorant of the scriptures and the Dharma. Suddenly, on the eighth day of the second month, both disappeared, and after three days, they returned, roughly describing having seen a Buddha. On the fifteenth day of the ninth month, they disappeared again, returning after ten days, speaking a foreign language, reciting scriptures and Sanskrit texts, and meeting monks from the Western Regions, immediately understanding each other. In the following year, on the fifteenth day of the first month, they suddenly disappeared again. Farmers in the fields reported seeing them being carried away by the wind and ascending into the sky. Their parents cried out in fear and worshipped gods to seek blessings. After a month, they returned, shaved their heads to become nuns, dressed in monastic robes, and carried their hair back. They said for themselves that they had seen a Buddha and nuns, and the Buddha said, "Your past-life karma has led you to be my disciples." He raised his hands and touched their heads, and their hair fell out. He gave them Buddhist names: the elder was named Fa Yuan, and the younger was named Fa Cai. When they were about to be sent back, he said, "You can build a temple, and I will give you the scriptures and teachings." After returning home, they immediately destroyed the altars for spirits, built a temple, and at night, they chanted the scriptures together. At dusk, there was often a five-colored light, flowing over the peaks and ridges, like lamps and candles. From then on, their demeanor became elegant and refined, their speech well-structured and correct, surpassing the style of the capital. Governor Wei Lang, Kong Mo, and others welcomed and supported them, hearing their discussions, greatly respecting and admiring them. Thus, everyone in the village came to respect the law. *Julu 5, also 22*.

Cao Yuqiu, courtesy name Shuda, was from Taiyuan. He served as the governor of Fuling and lost control of the city in the ninth year of the Yuanjia era (432 AD). As a result, he was imprisoned in a criminal court, where he was chained with a heavy lock that was firmly secured. Cao Yuqiu had always been diligent in his practice, and upon being imprisoned, he focused his efforts even more intently. There were over a hundred prisoners in the jail, many of whom were suffering from hunger. Whenever Cao Yuqiu ate, he would share his food with them. Each day, he would fast and meditate on the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara with great sincerity. One night, he dreamed of ascending to a high seat and encountering a monk who handed him a scroll of scripture, which was inscribed with the title "The Chapter of Enlightened Conduct and the Names of the Bodhisattvas." Cao Yuqiu read through it, but he could not remember the name of the first Bodhisattva, only recognizing the second as Avalokiteshvara and the third as Mahasthamaprapta. In the dream, he also saw another monk holding a wheel, who said, "This is the wheel of the Five Paths." Upon waking, he found that the lock on his chains had broken. Cao Yuqiu realized that his spiritual practice had increased his supernatural power, and he felt a deep sense of determination. He then proceeded to fix the lock himself, and after three days, he was pardoned. This account is recorded in the 23rd chapter of the *Jewel Forest*.

A Man Named Liu Ling from the Song Dynasty Liu Ling, a man from the Song Dynasty, was of unknown origin and resided in the Jinling Eastern Road City Garrison. He followed the law to some extent and built a small temple in his home, where he held periodic vegetarian feasts and gatherings. On the 27th day of the third month in the ninth year of the Yuanjia era, his father died suddenly. The shamans and diviners all said that his family would have three more deaths. A Taoist priest named Wei Po from a neighboring house, who often used talismans and deceived the people in the garrison, told Liu Ling, "Your family's misfortune has not ended because you worship foreign gods. If you serve the great Tao, you will surely receive blessings. If you do not change your mind, your entire family will be destroyed." Liu Ling then invited the Taoist priest and stopped following the law. Wei Po said, "You should burn the scriptures and statues, and the disaster will be eliminated." Liu Ling then closed the temple doors and set fire to them. The fire burned for a whole day, but what was burned was only the buildings. The scriptures, statues, and banners remained undamaged. In the middle of the night, the statue itself seemed to set fire again. At that time, there were about twenty Taoist priests. Some were also afraid of the miraculous events and secretly left. Wei Po and his disciples still persisted in their efforts. They went around with disheveled hair and weapons, claiming to drive the Buddha back to the foreign land, not allowing him to stay in China, where he would harm the people. That night, it seemed as if someone was beating Liu Ling. He fell to the ground, and his family helped him up, showing that he was still alive. He became paralyzed and could no longer move. The Taoist priest Wei Po developed a boil inside his body, producing two liters of pus daily, and he died within a month. His fellow practitioners also contracted leprosy. His neighbor, the governor of Dong'an, Shui Qiuhe, passed this story on to Dongyang Wuyi. Many people also saw these events. This story is recorded in "Jade Forest, Volume 62."

Ma Qianbo, from Langzhong, Bashu, was a devout follower of Buddhism. He once served as the magistrate of Xuanhan County. In the twelfth year of the Yuanjia era (435 AD), during the night of July, he had a dream: he saw three figures at the edge of the sky, each over two zhang tall, dignified and beautiful, descending from the clouds to observe. There were many heavenly musicians and dancers filling the space. From the air, they spoke to him: "Your calamity is in Jingchu. On the day of the Earth Tiger, August 4th, if you stay in the mountains and lakes, the calamity will be eliminated. If you observe fasting and purification, you can also be saved. If you miss this period, you will attain enlightenment." He saw eight familiar people, including Yang Xian, all bound with shackles. He also saw the Taoist priest Hu Liao, half of whose body was in the ground. The gods in the sky and at the edge of the sky all recorded the years and months when the eight people would die. They only spoke to Hu Liao: "If you can perform good deeds, you can extend your life." Yang Xian and the others all died as predicted. Hu Liao became even more fearful and devoted to the law, living in the mountains and working diligently.

Qianbo later served as the Western Cao Assistant of Liangzhou, as cited in the Guangji with the term 'Cao', referring to the state governor Xiao Sihua. Xiao was reassigned as a traveling military advisor. Qianbo heard the words of Jingchu, and was greatly afraid, requesting Xiao to resign and planning to go to Mount Heng, but Xiao strongly refused. The year was the 15th year of the Wu Yin period. In late June, he fell ill, and by August 4th, his condition was critical, but he held on. That evening, he suddenly saw clearly, far to the west, three people, tall, about two zhang. The first person was dressed in fine clothes, with hanging hair, and a round, bright halo on top of his head. The next two had golden, radiant features and dignified, complete demeanor, standing in the air, several ren away from the ground. Qianbo carefully observed, and it was still what he had dreamed of before. Soon they disappeared, and the lingering fragrance took a long time to fade. Everyone living there, regardless of age, smelled the fragrance, as a result, he sweated, and his condition improved slightly. Qianbo's dwelling was humble and shabby, at that moment, he felt himself in a hall, with corridors and walls shining with light, all adorned with treasures. Thus, all his ailments were cured. *Zhu Lin* 32, *Guang Ji* 113.

A Buddhist Monk Named Zhi Huaiqing from the Song Dynasty, a native of Yangzhou, was devout and knowledgeable. In the twelfth year of the Yuanchi era (435 AD), there was a great flood in Jingzhou and Yangzhou, submerging the rivers and mountains as one. Zhi Huaiqing was about to enter Mount Lu, and his small boat was caught in a sudden storm while others had already found shelter. His boat was drifting in the middle of the river, facing strong winds and waves, and it seemed destined to sink. Zhi Huaiqing focused his mind and thoughts, reciting the *Sutra of the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara*. People on the shore saw his boat defying the wind and currents, as if pulled by dozens of people, and it eventually reached the shore safely, with all aboard unharmed. This story is recorded in *Zhu Lin* (Chapter 65). Ge Jizhi, a native of Jurong, was a descendant of Ge Hong, a famous alchemist and Taoist scholar. His wife, Ji, from the same province, was graceful and virtuous. Ge Jizhi followed Taoist practices, and Ji did the same, but she had a particular interest in Buddhism and remained sincere in her faith. In the thirteenth year of the Yuanchi era (436 AD), while weaving, Ji suddenly felt a sense of clarity and peace. She looked up and saw the Buddha of Infinite Life, along with canopies and banners, overshadowing the Milky Way. She was delighted and thought, "The sutras say the Buddha of Infinite Life, is this him?" She then bowed her head and face in reverence. Ge Jizhi, seeing her devotion, got up and went to her. Ji showed him the direction of the Buddha, and Ge Jizhi also saw the Buddha's half-body and the various canopies and banners, which soon disappeared. The sky and sun became colorful, and many people in the community saw the same phenomenon. After a couple of meals, the event gradually subsided. From then on, many people in the community turned to Buddhism. This story is recorded in *Zhu Lin* (Chapter 15, *Guangji* 114).

Nun Hui Mu of the Song dynasty, surnamed Fu, entered the monastic life at the age of eleven, received and upheld the lesser precepts, and dwelled in the Zui Tun Temple in Liang Prefecture. She began reading the *Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra* and recited two scrolls daily. Her teacher, Hui Chao, once built a scripture hall. When Hui Mu went to pay homage, she often saw a monk in the northeast corner of the room, golden in complexion and wearing black robes, whose feet did not touch the ground. At night, while lying down and reciting and studying, she dreamed of the Western Paradise, where she saw a bathing pool filled with lotus flowers and various beings born through transformation seated in rows. There was a large lotus flower, empty and without anyone. Hui Mu wanted to climb onto it, strained and pulled with effort, and unconsciously recited the sutra. The sound was loud and resonant. Her mother thought she was having a nightmare and got up to call to her. Hui Mu's mother was very old and had no teeth left. Hui Mu always chewed food and fed her mother, but because it was past noon, she could not perform a clean rinse, so she did not receive the full precepts as she approached adulthood. After her mother passed away, Hui Mu cleared the grass and prepared a platform, inviting a teacher to receive the precepts.

A Sudden Vision and Its Aftermath Suddenly, at the altar, he saw the heavens and earth shimmering, all in a golden hue. Looking up to the southwest, he saw a celestial being dressed in a robe of red and yellow, appearing sometimes near and sometimes far, eventually disappearing. All that he saw of the supernatural, he kept secret and did not speak of to others. Brother Mu, who had become a monk, heard of this and wanted to know more, so he deceived him, saying, "You have practiced the Way for many years, but have achieved nothing. Soon you will be able to grow your hair, and then you should leave the monastery." Mu was very afraid upon hearing this and thought it was true, so he roughly described what he had seen. Only the nun Jingcheng, who heard of his virtue, approached him with familiarity and skillfully asked, so he told her everything. Later, Mu and his peers worshipped the Buddha of Infinite Life, and because he fell to the ground and did not rise, they thought he had fallen asleep. They kicked him to ask, but Mu did not answer. Nun Jingcheng then persistently asked again, and Mu said, "When I was lying on the ground, I dreamed of going to the Pure Land and meeting the Buddha, who spoke to me a small sutra, which I have already received in four volumes. I was awakened by being kicked, and I deeply regret it." Mu was sixty-nine years old in the 14th year of the Yuanjia era. This account is from the 15th volume of the "Jewel Forest."

A Monk Named Seng Yu of the Song Dynasty Seng Yu was a monk from Yuhang, Wu Prefecture, originally surnamed Zhou. At the age of twenty, he became a monk and was known for his mastery of spiritual principles. He diligently practiced ascetic practices and remained steadfast throughout his life. In the fifteenth year of the Yuanjia era (438 AD), he visited and stayed at Mount Lu, where he met fellow monks such as Tan Wen and Hui Guang, all of whom maintained pure and upright conduct and favored seclusion. Together, they built a dwelling on the sunny side of the mountain, which is now known as the Zhaoyin Monastery. Seng Yu often believed that being trapped in the cycle of rebirth was due to the nature of body and mind. When the mind is exhausted, the body should also be reduced. He thought that the practice of self-immolation, similar to the self-sacrifice of a medicine king, was not far-fetched. Therefore, he repeatedly made vows and began to agree to self-immolation. On the third day of the sixth month in the second year of the Xiaojian era (455 AD), he intended to fulfill his vow. Monks and laypeople came to witness the event, and carriages and horses filled the roads. Seng Yu led the congregation in chanting and taught the precepts. On that day, dark clouds were about to bring rain. Seng Yu made a solemn vow with great emotion: "If my wish is granted, the sky will be clear and bright; if my wish is not granted, it would be better for it to rain. So that these four groups of people know that the divine response is not hidden." After he spoke, soon after, the clouds cleared and the sky became bright. When the flames reached him, he had his hands joined and focused, and a purple aura rose into the air, separately visible outside the smoke, lasting for a while before disappearing. Fourteen days later, in the room where he lived, two cedars grew. The roots and branches were lush and uniform in size, straight and tall, piercing the rafters, thus becoming great trees. Those who understand the meaning believe they are the sacred sala trees, shining brightly in Nirvana, a sign of his near attainment. He was then called the Double Cedar Monk. Zhang Bian of Wu Prefecture, at the time the Chief Clerk of the Southern Pacification Department, witnessed the event personally and wrote a detailed account and praise.

In the sixteenth year of the Yuanjia era, a man named Ruan Zizong from Hedong followed the governor of Zhongli, Ruan Yin, to a remote military post. Gai Bianting, a local official, accompanied them. They reached a house where Ruan Zizong suddenly fell into a dazed state, as if he were asleep, and did not regain consciousness. The locals thought he was dead and carried him outside to prepare for burial. However, after a night, Ruan Zizong was able to speak again. He recounted that he had been taken by about a hundred people, who bound him and led him away for dozens of miles until they reached a Buddhist temple. The monks there were engaged in offerings and worship, just as they would in the world. One monk said to him, "You liked fishing and hunting in the past, and now you should receive your retribution."

A monk took the skin of Zhi Zong, cut and prepared it as if treating various beasts, then placed it back into deep water, pulled it out with a hook, sliced and chopped it into pieces resembling sashimi, boiled it in a large pot and roasted it over a fire. Initially, everything was completely ruined, but then it was restored, causing intense physical and emotional suffering that lasted for three times before stopping. The monk asked if Zhi Zong wanted to live, and Zhi Zong knelt and begged for mercy. The monk made him squat and poured water over him, saying that each pour would remove 500 sins. Zhi Zong desperately requested more water to be poured, and the monk replied, "Only three times is enough." Seeing several ants, the monk pointed and said, "These are small creatures, but they should not be killed, let alone larger ones. Fish and meat should only be eaten if they die naturally. On days of religious gatherings, wear new clothes, or wash old clothes if new ones are not available." Zhi Zong then asked, "Among the three travelers, why was only I affected by this suffering?" The monk replied, "The other two knew their sins and rewards but deliberately committed them; you, being ignorant, did not understand the consequences, so I warned you." As a result, Zhi Zong recovered and was able to stand up after several days. From then on, he stopped fishing. This story is from *Zhulin 64*.
A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

Cheng Dedu of the Song Dynasty was from Wuchang. His father, Dao Hui, was the governor of Guangzhou, and Cheng Dedu served as an assistant military officer under the Prince of Linchuan. At the time, he was in Xunyang, where he had a nest in his house. One night, the inside of the house suddenly became illuminated. A small child emerged from the nest, about a foot tall, clean and distinct. The child walked up to Cheng Dedu's bed and said, "In two years, you will attain the way of immortality." The child then suddenly disappeared. Cheng Dedu found this event very mysterious and extraordinary. In the 17th year of the Yuanchang era (440 AD), he followed the Prince of Linchuan to Guangling, where he met the Zen master Shi Daogong. He began to study Zen Buddhism and gained significant understanding. By the spring of the 19th year (442 AD), in a vacant room in his family's home in Wuchang, a particularly fragrant aroma suddenly appeared and spread throughout the streets. People from the entire region came to see the phenomenon, and the fragrance lasted for three days before dissipating. This event is recorded in the 28th chapter of the *Jade Forest*.

Liu Chenzhi, a native of Pei Commandery in the Song Dynasty, once encountered a monk in Guangling. The monk told Chenzhi, "You have an illness, but you will not die. You can prepare a meal worth one or two hundred coins to feed the monks, and then you will be free from this illness." Chenzhi always disbelieved in the Dharma and had a rebellious heart. The monk said, "You should add faith, do not be angry." They were twenty steps apart, and suddenly the monk disappeared. Chenzhi suffered from a severe illness for seven days and was on the verge of death. On the ninth day, during the day, he saw a five-story pagoda in his heart, as if in a dream but not quite. There were about twenty monks circling the pagoda in reverence. Because of this, he woke up and gained great benefit, and his illness gradually improved. Later, while living in the capital, a monk suddenly appeared, whom he did not recognize, and entered directly. He said, "You have a karmic connection with the Dharma, why are you not diligent?" Chenzhi then recounted his previous encounter, and the monk replied, "This is Venerable Punahala." After speaking, the monk left, and his destination was unknown. In the summer of the 17th year of the Yuanjia era, Chenzhi saw from afar at the entrance of the Huaiwang Monastery in Guangling a large number of banners and canopies but no visible images. He rushed to see it, but by the time he reached the gate, everything had vanished. This story is recorded in the *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest), volume thirty-six.

During the 19th year of the reign of Emperor Wu of Liu Song, Fu Wanshou, a man from Pingchang, was serving as an assistant military officer in the guard department in Guangling. After his leave of absence ended, he returned by boat, crossing the river at the beginning of the fourth watch of the night. At the start of the crossing, the river was calm and smooth, but a strong wind suddenly arose in the middle of the river, like an arrow. The night was extremely dark, and no one knew where to go. Fu Wanshou had been diligently observing the precepts and devoted his mind entirely to the Bodhisattva Guanyin, without a moment's interruption in his recitation. Soon, he and several people on the boat saw a light on the northern bank, which looked like a campfire. They were delighted and said, "This must be the fire of Ouyang!" They turned the boat towards it and arrived before dawn. When they asked the people there, they all said that no one had lit a fire the previous night. Only then did they realize the power of the divine, and they held a vegetarian feast. This story is recorded in the 27th chapter of the *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest). During the 19th year of the reign of Emperor Wu of Liu Song, Gu Mai, a man from Wu County, was also serving as an assistant military officer in the guard department. He returned from the capital to Guangling, departing from Shitoucheng and heading upstream along the lake. A northerly wind suddenly arose, causing the river to surge. The wind did not subside, and the boatmen were eager to proceed. When they reached the middle of the river, the waves were becoming increasingly strong. Gu Mai was traveling alone in a single boat, feeling worried and without a plan. He began to recite the *Sutra of the Bodhisattva Guanyin*, completing it about ten times. The wind gradually calmed, and the waves also became smaller. As they continued down the river, they frequently smelled a strange fragrance that did not dissipate. Gu Mai found this fragrance pleasing and continued to recite the sutra without stopping. Thus, he was able to cross the river safely. This story is also recorded in the 27th chapter of the *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest).

A Monk from the Qin Region Named Dao Jiong The hometown and clan of Monk Dao Jiong are already recorded in previous accounts. In the eighteenth year of Emperor Yao Xing's reign (396 CE), Master Dao Yi sent him to Mount Huo in Henan to collect cave-dwelling snails. He traveled with three fellow students, including Dao Lang. Holding a torch, they explored a cave for about three miles, where they encountered a deep stream and crossed it by placing a log across. Monk Dao Jiong was the first to cross, but his companions fell off the log and drowned. At that moment, the fire went out, plunging them into total darkness. Dao Jiong felt all hope was lost and wept bitterly. Still, he called out with all his heart to Avalokiteshvara, vowing that if he were granted a way out, he would offer a meal for a hundred people to express gratitude to the Bodhisattva's power. After one night, he saw a faint but clear light, shaped like a firefly. In an instant, the entire cave was illuminated. He then saw a path and emerged from the cave. From this experience, his faith and understanding deepened, and he frequently witnessed miraculous phenomena. In the nineteenth year of Emperor Wen's reign (442 CE), Prince of Linchuan, serving as governor of Guangling (modern Yangzhou), invited Dao Jiong to reside and provide for him. In the ninth month of that year, in the western study, a ten-day fasting observance dedicated to Avalokiteshvara was held. After nine days, at the fourth watch of the night, all the monks were asleep, but Dao Jiong got up to pay respects and was about to resume meditation. Suddenly, he saw countless monks on the walls, all appearing with their upper bodies visible. Among them was a Buddha with a coiled topknot, clearly visible. There was also a tall man, wearing a flat-topped hat, a robe, and trousers, holding a long sword, with an extremely fierce appearance. He offered incense to Dao Jiong, but the monk refused to accept it. The monks on the wall said, "Master Jiong, you should accept the incense to protect the host." Soon, the vision disappeared, and at that moment, Dao Jiong could not see any of the gathered monks, only the statue of Shakyamuni Buddha that was placed there. This account is found in the sixty-fifth volume of the "Jewel Forest" (珠林).

Buddhist Nun Tanhui from the Song Dynasty was from Chengdu, Sichuan, originally surnamed Qingyang and named Baiyu. At the age of seven, she enjoyed sitting in meditation. Whenever she meditated, she would attain a state of mind, but her thoughts were not fully clear, and she also considered it to be a dream. Once she slept with her sister, and at night she entered a deep meditative state. Her sister found her at the corner of the screen, and her body was like wood and stone, with no breath. Her sister was greatly surprised and alarmed, and called her family. They all held and supported her, until dawn when she came to. They ran to consult sorcerers, who all said it was due to spirits. By the age of eleven, a foreign monk named Yeshe came to Sichuan. Tanhui asked for his opinion on what she saw. Yeshe, seeing that she had a natural aptitude for meditation, wanted to persuade her to become a nun. At the time, Tanhui was about to marry, and the wedding date was already set. Her legal marriage was not yet arranged, and upon hearing this, her family secretly brought her back to the temple. When the family found out, they tried to force her into marriage. Tanhui refused to go and made a deep vow: "If my spiritual heart is not fulfilled and I am forced to marry, I will throw myself into the fire to feed tigers, discard my impure form, and wish that the Buddhas of the ten directions witness my sincere heart." Governor Zhen Fachong, who believed and upheld the true Dharma, heard of Tanhui's aspirations and welcomed and met with her. He also summoned his officials and monks who had aspirations to question her. Tanhui spoke eloquently and without being defeated, and those present were impressed. Zhen then allowed her to leave her fiancé's family and enter the path of the Dharma. In the 19th year of the Yuanjia era, King Kang of Linchuan invited her to Guangling.

During the reign of Emperor Wen of Song, in the year 423 AD, Zhao Xi, a native of Huainan, served as an assistant in the military office. He suffered from a prolonged illness, deeply concerned that it would not be cured. He always sincerely turned to Buddhism in his heart. One night, he dreamed of a person with an exceptionally beautiful and noble appearance, resembling a deity, who appeared from the ceiling and gave him a small wrapped object and a razor. The figure said, "Take this medicine and use this razor, and your illness will surely be cured immediately." Zhao Xi was startled awake and indeed found the razor and medicine. He immediately took the medicine, and his illness was cured. He then left home to become a monk, named Xiu, and lived past eighty years old before passing away. This story is recorded in the "Jewel Forest" chapter twenty-two. In the Song dynasty, there was a monk named Shihuiquan, a Zen master from Liangzhou, who taught and instructed five hundred disciples. One of his disciples had a rather rough and violent temperament, and Shihuiquan often disdained him. Later, the disciple suddenly claimed to have attained the three fruits of enlightenment. Shihuiquan, however, did not believe him due to his lack of virtuous conduct and never accepted his claim. When Shihuiquan later fell ill, the disciple came to visit him at night. The door was still closed as usual, and Shihuiquan was quite surprised and wanted to test him again. He told the disciple to come back the next evening. Shihuiquan then secretly sealed the windows and doors. That night, the disciple arrived in the middle of the night and went directly to the bed, saying to Shihuiquan, "Venerable one, you can believe me now." He then said, "After your passing, you will be reborn into a Brahmin family."

Quan said, "I practice meditation and accumulate merit; how could I not be reborn in a higher realm?" The disciple replied, "Venerable one, your faith in the path is not firm, and you have not completely abandoned external studies. Even though you have merit, you cannot transcend to higher realms. If you hold a grand assembly and offer food to a sage, you can attain enlightenment." Quan then held the assembly. The disciple further advised, "You can offer the Sanghali robes as alms; if anyone needs one, do not choose based on age." After the assembly, when distributing the robes, a novice monk approached Quan and asked for one. Quan thought he was one of his disciples and said, "I intended to offer it to a holy monk; how could I give it to you?" Recalling his earlier words that he should not choose based on people, Quan happily gave the robe away. On another day, Quan saw this novice monk and asked, "Wasn't the robe you received not too big?" The novice monk replied, "Not only did I not receive a robe, but there were also some reasons, and I am ashamed that I was not invited to the gathering." Quan finally realized that the previous novice monk was a manifestation of a sage. The disciple passed away after a long time; at the time of his passing, there were no other unusual phenomena, only around the grave, there was sometimes a white light. Quan was still alive in the 20th year of the Yuanjia era, residing in Jiuquan. This is recorded in the 19th chapter of the *Zhu Lin*.

Hu of Song, a native of Chang'an, had a brother who died several years ago. In the 23rd year of the Yuanjia era (447 AD), the spirit of the deceased brother suddenly appeared in the form of a living person and returned home. He reproached Hu for lacking in carefulness and propriety, and for neglecting household affairs, and punished him with five strokes. People around and neighbors all heard his words and the sound of the strokes, and also saw the marks of the strokes, but they did not see his form; only Hu could still touch him. The spirit said to Hu, "I should not have died, but the divine way required me to calculate the records of the ghosts. Now I am accompanied by many officials and soldiers, fearing to frighten or harm the village, so I did not bring them in." Hu also saw many ghosts gathered outside, causing a commotion. Soon after, the spirit said goodbye and left, saying, "I will return again next year on the seventh day of the seventh month, and I want to take you along to tour the underworld, to make you understand the retribution of sin and virtue. No need to spend money on preparations; if you wish, just bring tea." On the appointed day, he indeed returned and told Hu's family, "I will now take Hu to see the sights, and when we are done, I will send him back; there is no need to worry." Hu immediately lay down on the bed, as if he had passed away. The spirit then took Hu to see all the mountains, and they saw all kinds of ghosts and monsters. Finally, they reached Mount Song. Various ghosts met Hu, and there were preparations for food. The rest of the flavors were not different from those in the world, but only the ginger was very crisp and delicious.

Hu wished to return home with something in mind, but those around him laughed at him and said, "You can only eat here; you cannot take it with you." Hu then came upon a place where the house was grand and spacious, the furnishings were finely arranged, and two young monks lived there. Hu visited them, and the monks prepared a variety of fruits and betel nuts for him. After wandering for a long time, Hu fully witnessed the rewards and punishments of sin and virtue, as well as the sufferings and joys of life, and then he bade farewell and returned home. His uncle said to Hu, "Since you have now come to know that virtue can be cultivated, why should you remain at home? Whitefoot Alayan is strict in precepts and high in moral conduct; you can serve him as a teacher." The monk from Chang'an, whose feet were bare, was thus called Whitefoot Alayan, and he was highly respected by the Wei barbarians, who regarded him as a teacher. Hu then followed this advice and stayed in the temple, where he saw the young monk from Mount Song among the students. Hu was greatly surprised and talked about how long it had been since they last met, asking when he had arrived. The two monks replied, "I originally lived in this temple, but I don't remember meeting you in the past." Hu then recounted his encounter with the young monk from Mount Song, but this monk said, "You are mistaken; how could there be such a thing?" The next day, the two monks left without further ado. Hu then told all the monks about what he had seen at Mount Song in the past; they were all astonished and immediately searched for the two monks, but they could not be found. Hu then realized that they were divine beings. At the end of the Yuanjia era, a monk from Chang'an named Shitan came to tour the south and recounted the same story. This is recorded in the sixth chapter of the "Julei."

During the reign of the Song Dynasty, the *Yingya Shenglan* records a man named Bian Yuezhi from Jiyin. He held a court title and lived in Chao Gou. At the age of fifty, he had no children, so his wife arranged for him to take a concubine. After many years, the concubine remained barren. Intending to pray for descendants, he recited the *Guanyin Sutra* a thousand times. The *Yingya Shenglan* records that he made a vow to recite the *Guanyin Sutra* a thousand times, nearly completing the recitation. Soon after, the concubine became pregnant and gave birth to a son. At the time, the *Yingya Shenglan* records that it was the year of the Earth Ox in the 28th year of the reign of Emperor Wen of Song (451 CE). During the reign of the Song Dynasty, a Buddhist monk named Shi Tandian, who was a layperson at the age of thirty, suddenly died of a severe illness. After seven days, he came back to life. He recounted that at the moment of death, he saw two people leading him away, sent to carry rice. There were about several thousand people carrying rice, day and night without rest. He saw two monks who said, "We are your original teachers of the Five Precepts," and came to console him. The monks led him to the official, saying that he was their disciple, and that he had no great sin, and the calculation of fate had not run out. He was immediately released and sent away. The two monks escorted Tandian back home, stayed on the roof of the house, and explained everything to him. They advised him to become a monk and diligently practice the Dharma. After finishing speaking, the monks descended from the roof, pushed Tandian onto the body's armpit, and thus he revived. He later became a monk, practiced for twenty years, and died in the 14th year of the reign of Emperor Wen of Song (437 CE).

Wēi Huái zhī, whose courtesy name was Yuán Zēng, was from Lángyá. His family had long been devoted to Confucianism and did not believe in Buddhism. He often said, "The body and spirit both perish; how can there be three temporal realms?" During the reign of Emperor Wen of Song (Yùnjiā era), he served as the magistrate of Dānyáng. After ten years, he fell ill and appeared to die, but after a short while, he briefly revived. At that time, the magistrate of Jiàn Kāng, Hè Dàolì, came to visit him as he lay on his deathbed. Wēi Huái zhī spoke to Hè Dàolì, saying, "I now know that Buddhism is not empty or false; the spirit continues to exist after death, and there is indeed evidence of this." Hè Dàolì replied, "Your Excellency, you have always held different views in the past. What have you seen now that makes you change your mind?" Wēi Huái zhī, gathering his strength, answered, "The spirit is indeed not exhausted; one cannot help but believe in Buddhism." With these words, he passed away. The *Zūlín* (Volume 79), *Guǎngjì* (Volume 99). During the reign of Emperor Wen of Song, there was a monk named Huì Hé, a resident of the temple in the capital city. During the turmoil of the Yìjiā era, Huì Hé was still a layperson and served under the command of Lǐu Hú. Lǐu Hú once sent dozens of soldiers on a reconnaissance mission to the east, and Huì Hé was among them. They reached Qièzhǔ, but encountered the central government’s troops marching westward. The reconnaissance force was scattered, and everyone fled into the wilderness. Huì Hé managed to escape and reached the outskirts of Xīnlín. There, he saw an old farmer whose clothes were tattered. Huì Hé exchanged his complete trousers and jacket for the old man’s clothes, then carried a basket and a burden, appearing like a farmer. At that time, the patrol forces were searching for the scattered reconnaissance soldiers. Seeing Huì Hé’s appearance, they suspected him and questioned him. Huì Hé gave vague and inconsistent answers, and as a result, he was beaten and was about to be executed. As Huì Hé fled, he constantly recited the *Guānshìyīn Sūtra*. When the executioner was about to strike, Huì Hé’s prayers grew more fervent. The executioner’s sword slipped three times, and each time it fell short. Startled, the executioner released him. Huì Hé then left the military and entered monastic life, eventually achieving great spiritual attainments. The *Zūlín* (Volume 27).

Cui Huayan, a monk at the Changsha Temple in Jiangling, was a disciple of Huayan, who was skilled in Zen meditation and was known as a Zen master. Originally, Cui Huayan was Huayan's servant, named Huang Qian. When he was twenty years old, Huayan would often enter meditation and see in a vision that Huang Qian's previous life was his own teacher. Therefore, Huayan decided to ordain Huang Qian as a disciple. Cui Huayan often stayed at the home of Yang Daochan in the western part of Jiangling. He practiced the Banzhou method, involving continuous walking meditation, for over a year, and as a result, he experienced significant spiritual transformations. Sometimes, in a single day, he would attend more than ten religious ceremonies, continuing his spiritual practices throughout the day and night, and everyone who saw him would see Huang Qian. People began to respect and consider him enlightened. In the second year of the Xiaojian era, Cui Huayan announced his impending death, telling Yang Daochan, "Tomorrow night, I will pass away at your place." On that day, Yang Daochan prepared by setting up the Eight Precepts and lighting lamps all night.

At the early and middle watches of the night, Huang Qian hesitated among the crowd on the road, resting peacefully without any difference; after the fourth watch, he claimed to be tired and went to sleep, his appearance gradually changing, and in a short while, it was over. The environment was set up for a three-seven斋 (a type of ritual or ceremony), a stupa was built, and it still exists today. After his death, for a long time, he appeared in the Many Treasures Temple, telling the monk Tanxun, "Next year on February 23rd, I will be welcomed by the heavenly beings together." After speaking, he left. Tanxun immediately set up a ninety-day斋 (ritual) in the Chan room of Changsha, offering his body as a sacrifice until that day, suffering from lack of energy, he knew he would die soon, greatly inviting both monks and laypeople, and held a grand Dharma assembly. In the third watch of the night, he called out to the monks, "Did you hear or see anything?" The monks did not feel any difference, but Tanxun said, "There was music playing in the air, and the incense smoke was very unusual, the covenant of Huang Qian has arrived." The monks just returned to the hall and took their seats, but Tanxun had already passed away. *Zhu Lin* 97, 19 In the fourth year of Daming during the reign of Empress Dowager Lu of the Song Dynasty, a statue of the Bodhisattva Samantabhadra riding a white elephant in a precious carriage was placed in the Zongxing Chan room, and a lecture was held in the temple. On the eighth day of the tenth month of that year, after the斋 (ritual) was completed and the assembly was disbanded, there were two hundred monks present. At that time, the temple was just being constructed, the emperor paid great attention to it, visiting frequently, often several times a week, the monks were diligent and orderly, and the guards were strict. On that day, the names of the monks were determined, and they had been seated for a long time when suddenly one monk appeared among them, with a graceful and dignified appearance, causing the entire hall to be startled. The斋 (ritual) host spoke with him, exchanging over a hundred words, and then he suddenly disappeared. The guests at the banquet all recognized him as a divine being. *Zhu Lin* 17

During the reign of Emperor Tai Ming of the Song Dynasty, there was a high-ranking Buddhist monk named Dao Wen, who resided in Lengling County. Having witnessed the Empress Dowager's wise insight and profound clarity, her divine signs and harmonious mysteries, and the purification of thoughts in a clean realm and delving into the highest realms of the heart, he naturally achieved reverberating eloquence in the center and engaged in affairs of the divine and the sacred. Thus, he innovatively thought and meticulously carved, extracting divine beauty to create the splendid statue of Samantabhadra, using treasures from the universe and exquisite adornments. The lecture and fasting sessions he organized continued until the eighth day of the month, with donations limited and names registered and determined, leading participants in order to the seats, with the number remaining constant. Halfway through the sutra recitation, when the sun reached the Kunwu Mountains, a strange monk suddenly appeared among the attendees, with a dignified and composed demeanor and a radiant and noble appearance, attracting the attention of all the monks. The host asked, "What is your name, revered one?" The monk replied, "My name is Hui Ming." The host then asked where he lived, and the monk answered, "I come from Tian'an Temple." As they spoke, the monk suddenly disappeared. The hall was filled with shock, and all the guests were solemn and thoughtful, thinking it was a sign of good fortune and mysterious responses and explanations. They imagined the purple mountain could be seen and the splendid platform was not far. It is said that sincere devotion can move the stars and change the constellations, and a clear heart can move mountains and open springs. Given the imperial virtue that encompasses the universe and the widespread and harmonious imperial achievements, benevolence reaches far, and principles extend beyond the visible world, thus, the great scholars of the court can represent the era of Great Ming, encouraging the manifestation of the wonderful body and seeing the dragon's flight room personally. As if saying, "Your Majesty's wisdom illuminates the sea and counties, bright as the sun and moon. Therefore, the name Hui Ming is given to a person, continuing the heavenly mandate and enduring forever. Therefore, the temple is named Tian'an." The divine foundation extends far, and the moral governance is solidifying, with all nine regions recognizing peace and all beings being joyful. Carefully listing words for the county to explain the heavenly blessings. Seventeenth Chapter of the Pearl Forest.

Jiang Xiaode, a man from Jiangling, served as a supervisor under the military governor Zhu Xunshi during the Song Dynasty. Young and trustworthy, he was diligent and meticulous beyond others, which greatly pleased Zhu Xunshi. Every time there was a legal matter, Zhu Xunshi would always instruct him to handle the affairs. In the late Ming Dynasty, Jiang Xiaode died of illness. In the third watch of the night, he was about to be buried when he suddenly revived. He said: "There was a messenger who claimed that he was summoned by the king." Jiang Xiaode followed him. After arriving, the king said, "You are diligent and careful, and you faithfully adhere to the great law. The emperor's decree and intent have specially appointed you, so you should quickly be reborn in a good place. But your life span is still long, so I have specially summoned you. You will now receive heavenly joy and happiness." Jiang Xiaode expressed gratitude and assent. The king said, "You may return home for now. Anything you wish to entrust or perform meritorious deeds, you should do it quickly, and return in seven days." Jiang Xiaode followed the instructions and returned home.

There is a place called Route One, where there is a small house that is extremely shabby and dilapidated. The monk "Nan Gong" encountered the new temple in front of this house. Since we are already acquainted, we exchanged greetings. The monk said, "Since I became a monk, I have never drunk alcohol, and I went to see Lan Gong, who strongly urged and pressured me to drink about one sheng. Because of this, I was summoned by the king. If I had not stayed here, I would have attained heaven, but now I am staying in this dilapidated house, and I will finally ascend three years later." Little De returned home to verify his words and immediately that evening, he hurriedly sent someone to inquire about Monk Nan. Indeed on that day, Monk Nan lay down to sleep at Lan Gong's place and died that evening. Little De was cured, and within seven days, a grand offering of merit was set up, but by the time, he died suddenly. Zhuxun was immediately exempted from military service. The two monks, Lan and Nan, both reside in the new temple, and Monk Nan practices with great diligence, unlike the other monks. This is recorded in "Zhu Lin" Volume 94.

During the reign of Emperor Gong, Shen Sengfu from Wu Prefecture, faced with famine in his hometown, went to Shan Yang in search of food. During the day, he would enter the fields to beg for food, and at night, he would stay near the temple. At that time, there were many small copper statues in the temples of Shan Yang. Shen Sengfu and several people from his hometown gradually stole these statues, eventually filling several bags and chests. They then took them back home and melted them down to make coins. When the theft was discovered, they were arrested and sent to the capital. On the boat, Shen Sengfu claimed to see someone burning him with fire. He screamed day and night, claiming that the pain was unbearable, and died before the official punishment could be carried out. His entire body burst open, as if burned. Zhu Hen from Wu Prefecture, who knew Shen Sengfu personally, witnessed the entire event. (From *Zhu Lin* 79 and *Guang Ji* 116) Nun Hui Yu, from Chang'an, was a diligent practitioner of the Dharma, well-versed in scriptures and precepts. One day, while at Xue Shangshu Temple in Chang'an, she saw red and white lights for over ten days. On the eighth day of the fourth month, a monk from Liu Chong Temple came to visit and found a golden statue of Maitreya, about a foot tall, where the light was. Hui Yu later moved south to Jiangling and stayed at Ling Shou Temple. In the fourteenth year of the Yuangang era, on a night in October, she saw a brilliant purple light rise from a tree in the temple grounds, illuminating the entire grove. She told her fellow monk Miao Guang about it, but he could not see it. Over the next twenty days, Hui Yu continued to see the light. Later, the temple master Fa Hong was building a meditation base under the tree when he found a golden seated statue, also about a foot tall. (From *Zhu Lin* 16)

A Man Named Fei Chongxian of Song Mr. Fei Chongxian of Song was from Wu County. In his youth, he had some faith in the Dharma, and by the age of thirty, his diligence and devotion became even more intense. In the third year of Tai Shi, he received the Bodhisattva precepts and stayed for meditation at the home of Xie Huaiyuan for twenty-four days, without rest day or night. Every time he listened to the scriptures, he would place a magpie-tail incense burner on his knees. According to the *Initial Learning Record* (卷二十五), it is recorded that Fei Chongxian, in his youth, believed in the Buddha and often placed a magpie-tail incense burner on his knees during his initial meditation retreat. On the third night of his retreat, he saw a person of extraordinary appearance and attire who came straight to take the incense burner away. Chongxian looked at the incense burner on his knees and found it still there. He looked more closely at the person and saw them take the incense burner away clearly. Chongxian then realized it was a supernatural phenomenon. He thought to himself that his clothes had just been washed and were completely clean, except for the spittoon near him. After he removed the spittoon, he saw the person return with the incense burner to sit in front of him. For a short while he still saw two incense burners, but then they merged into one. Thus, what the divine person took away was probably just a shadow of the incense burner. Chongxian also heard from others that there was a nun named Qinni at Fuyuan Temple who was diligent and had attained enlightenment. He was delighted to see her, but before he could go, he had already developed a strong attachment. Once, while staying for meditation at another's home, in the middle of the third watch of the night, he suddenly saw a nun with dignified appearance, wearing a dark red robe, standing upright in front of the meditation hall. She vanished after a meal's worth of time. When Chongxian later met this nun, her appearance and attire were exactly as he had seen from the window. This account is found in the *Jewel Forest* (卷二十四).

He Jingshu from the eastern sea region was devoted to Buddhism from a young age. During the reign of Emperor Wen (泰始 period), he followed the governor of Xiang Prefecture, Liu Yun (刘韫), in supervising Yingpu County. At the time, He Jingshu obtained some sandalwood and carved it into a statue. The statue was nearly complete, but it lacked the material for the halo. He Jingshu was very eager to complete the statue, but despite his efforts, he could not find the necessary material. While leaning against a table and dozing off, he saw a monk in a simple robe and holding a staff and alms bowl come to him. The monk said, "There is a paulownia shield in the house of the He family after the county, which is very suitable for the halo of the statue. The owner values it greatly, but it can be obtained with great effort." He Jingshu awoke and asked about the He family after the county, and indeed, there was such a family. He then requested to buy the shield. The He family said, "We do have this shield, and we value it greatly. How did you know about it, Your Excellency?" He Jingshu explained his dream, and the He family was amazed and impressed. They offered the shield to help complete the halo of the statue. Later, when He Jingshu served in the office of the prime minister, he had a dream in the middle of the night where the statue said, "A mouse is biting my foot." In the morning, he hurriedly returned to check on the statue, and indeed, a mouse was biting its foot.

After many years following his death, Yuan Bing, a man from Chen County, served as the magistrate of Linxiang at the end of the Tai Shi era. His friend Sima Xun, in a dream-like state just before dawn, saw Yuan Bing appear to him. Yuan Bing recounted their long separation and inquired about his well-being. He then said, "Throughout our lives, we often discussed and held the view that life is a race, while death is rest. Now I know for the first time that the extensive records and quotations containing the word 'know' are certainly not correct. I was always troubled by people in the world who are busy chasing after money and gold, exchanging gifts with each other. In the afterlife, this situation is also the same." Sima Xun asked, "Are the rewards and punishments for sins and virtues truly determined?" Yuan Bing replied, "As I saw it before, it does not fully agree with what is said in the scriptures. This is probably the sage's way of guiding and restraining. The general categories of good and evil are not very different. However, killing living beings is the most serious, and it must be strictly forbidden and not violated."

"Xun said, 'Your sign that you have shown me is truly difficult to put into words; you should inform the Shangshu (Minister of Works).' Bing replied, 'Very good, also please inform the Shangshu with respect.' At that time, the high-ranking official Jianmu Wang Gong (as recorded in *Guangji*), or Wang Sengqian as the *Guangji* states, was the Shangshu of the Ministry of Personnel, and Bing and Xun were among his honored guests, hence this conversation took place. They exchanged several hundred words before parting. Xun said, 'It has been a long time since we last met, and I have always thought of gathering and talking; it is very difficult to meet, so why don't you stay a little longer?' Bing said, 'I just came briefly! I cannot stay for long. Moreover, the words of such people cannot be fully understood.' So he left. Initially, when Bing arrived in the darkness, Xun did not know why but was able to see clearly. After Bing left, Xun got out of bed to see him off, and as he began to walk in sandals back into the darkness, he saw light about a foot long between Bing's feet, illuminating only his two feet, while the rest of the place was still dark. *Zhulin* 21, *Guangji* 326."

A monk named Daozhi from the Song dynasty, who was from the Northern Dabao temple, once served as a caretaker for the temple and pagoda. He secretly stole the canopy and other precious decorations, taking a great deal of them. Later, he stole the pearl from the Buddha statue's eyebrow. He then dug through the walls, as if it were an external thief, so the monks did not notice. After more than ten days, he fell ill and saw a strange person stabbing him with spears. The person came and went at times, and whenever they came, he would scream and blood would flow in response. At first, this happened once or twice a day, but later his illness worsened, and the number of stabbings increased, causing wounds all over his body. He moaned and could not stop. The monks of the same temple suspected he had committed a crime and wanted to perform a repentance ceremony. At first, he was still reluctant to speak, but as he was nearing death, two or three days later, he fully confessed and wept, begging for mercy. He said, "I am foolish and ignorant, believing there was no afterlife. Acting out of anger, I committed a sin, bringing this suffering upon myself. I suffer torture in life and am tormented in death. My ruined body, I only beg for pity and forgiveness."

Cen Xiu Yuan, a native of Yen-chuan, once served as the Western Censor of Xiangzhou and resided as a guest in Linxiang County. In his youth, he revered the Three Jewels of Buddhism, and even after reaching the age of sixty, he remained steadfast in his occupation. In the second year of the Yuexiu era of the Song Dynasty, during the seventh month, on an evening when he was lying down without sleeping, he reflected on the impermanence of life and death, pondering where he had come from. With a focused mind, he prayed, hoping for a dream that would convey a message. That evening was overcast, and the room was dark without any lighting. After a while, he saw a light like a firefly beside his pillow, shining brightly and then flying away. Soon, the entire room was illuminated, and the light reached the sky, as if it were daytime. Cen Xiu Yuan quickly sat up, folded his hands, and meditated. After a while, he saw a bridge and a pavilion about ten to fifteen feet above him, with red decorations standing in the sky. He did not realize how he was lifted, but he found himself sitting on the side of the bridge. He saw men and women on the bridge, coming and going, their clothing and appearance no different from ordinary people. An old woman, about thirty years old, appeared, wearing a green coat and a white cloth skirt, and stood to the left of Cen Xiu Yuan. After a while, another woman appeared, dressed entirely in white cloth, with a partial ring hairstyle, holding flowers and incense, and stood in front of him. She said to Cen Xiu Yuan, "If you want to see your previous life, I am it. Because I offered these flowers to the Buddha, I was reborn as you." She then pointed to the white-clad old woman and said, "This is also my previous life." After speaking, she left, and the bridge gradually disappeared. Cen Xiu Yuan suddenly realized he was back on the ground, and the light also disappeared. *Jade Forest* volume 32, *Record of the Broadly Recorded* volume 114.

A Monk Named Zhi Da from the Song Dynasty Zhi Da was a monk from the Suo Temple in Yizhou (present-day Chengdu, Sichuan). His behavior was somewhat conventional, but he was skilled in reciting scriptures and chants. At the age of twenty-three, in the third month of the Yuangui era of the Song dynasty (475 AD), he fell ill and died. His body remained warm, delaying the burial. After two days, he began to show signs of recovery. On the third day morning, he could speak and see again. He recounted his experience: when he first became ill, he saw two people dressed in yellow cloth trousers and jackets. One stood outside the door, and the other came directly to the bedside. The person said, "Reverend, you should leave; get off the bed." Zhi Da replied, "I am weak and frail; I cannot travel." The person then said, "You can ride in a carriage." As soon as he spoke, the carriage arrived. Zhi Da got into it, and his consciousness became unclear. He no longer saw his family's house or the carriage. Looking around, he only saw a desolate wilderness with difficult paths. The path showed him to climb and walk without rest. He reached a red gate with very ornate walls and doors. Zhi Da entered the hall.

In the hall, there was a noble person wearing a red cap and headband, sitting arrogantly on the bed with a severe and distant appearance, having a very dignified and imposing presence. About a hundred guards stood straight and orderly on either side, all holding red-handled swords. The noble person saw the monk and then adopted a serious expression, saying, "Why should a monk have many faults?" The monk replied, "Since gaining consciousness, I do not remember committing any sins." The noble person asked, "Have you neglected the recitation of the precepts?" The monk answered, "When I first received the full ordination, I indeed regularly recited them. In recent times, I have been attending sermons and reciting scriptures, thus there have been occasional lapses in reciting the precepts." The noble person said, "You may recite the scriptures instead." The monk then recited the Lotus Sutra three times and stopped. The noble person instructed those in charge, "You may send him to a bad place, but do not make it too harsh." Two people led the monk away, traveling for dozens of miles, and gradually heard a loud and chaotic noise, while the road ahead became darker.

markdown Next, he reached a gate, towering several dozen feet high, very dark and black, indicating it was made of iron. The wall was also similar in appearance. Da thought to himself: "The scriptures speak of hell, this must be it." He became greatly terrified. He regretted not cultivating virtue and good deeds in his lifetime. As he entered the gate, the noise grew louder. After a while, upon quiet listening, he realized it was the sound of people calling out. Inside the gate, it grew darker, and he could no longer see anything. The firelight flickered on and off. He saw several people bound and being led forward, behind them were several people holding something, and blood flowed like a spring. One of them was Da's aunt. They saw each other and wanted to speak, but someone dragged him away so quickly that he had no time to speak. After entering the gate for about two hundred steps, he saw an object shaped like a grain silo, about ten feet tall. Two people grabbed Da and threw him onto the silo. Inside the silo was fire, which burned Da's body. Half of his body was burned to a crisp, and the pain was unbearable. He fell from the silo to the ground and fainted for a long time. Two people took Da away again.

He saw more than ten iron cauldrons, all boiling with criminals. People were inside the cauldrons, rising and submerging with the boiling. By the side of the cauldrons, people pierced them with something — some climbed out of the cauldrons, their eyes swollen and boiling, their tongues protruding more than a foot, their flesh entirely charred yet still not dead. All the cauldrons were full, except for one that remained empty. Two men told Da, "A superior person should enter here at once." When Da heard these words, his liver and gall dropped to the ground. He then pleaded, "Please listen to me, a poor monk. Once I have paid homage to the Buddha, I will sincerely bow, hoping to be spared this suffering." He knelt on the ground for a meal's time, his prayers and repentance especially intense. Afterward, looking around, he saw nothing else, only a plain with lush trees and clear scenery. Yet the two men still led Da forward, until they reached the base of a small, high building. Inside, there was just enough space for a person to sit. The figure there said to Da, "The monk is now receiving a lighter retribution — this is indeed something to be glad of." Suddenly, Da did not realize he had returned to his original body. Today, Da still lives at So Temple, his fasting more resolute, his meditation and chanting more steadfast. *Zhu Lin* 90.

In the Song Dynasty, there was a man named Yuan Kuo, whose courtesy name was Sishu, and he was from Chen Prefecture. During the reign of Emperor Yuan of Song (472-477), he served as the magistrate of Wu Prefecture. He fell ill for a short time and appeared to be dead, but his breath was not completely gone. All the funeral preparations were ready, and they waited until the preparations were complete before embalming him. After three days, he was able to move and blink. He recounted that a messenger came and called him, and Kuo followed him. Upon arrival, they saw a large city with high and orderly towers and battlements, and grand and beautiful stairs and gates. Once he was invited to enter, the host sat facing south, with a solemn and majestic appearance adorned with a crown. A person with a knife pointed at Kuo to sit, and once seated, warm and cool dishes were served, including wine, roasted meat, fruits, zongzi (sticky rice dumplings), pickled vegetables, and other dishes. Kuo tried all the food, which had various types and flavors, no different from what is found in the world. After several rounds of wine, the host said to Kuo, "My position is humble and unfortunate, lacking in duties. Because of your sharp talent, I wish to invite you. Can you consider it?" Kuo understood that this was a reference to the afterlife and firmly declined, saying, "My humble position is not suitable. My family is poor and has few members, and my brothers are scattered in both public and private life. I beg for your mercy and release." The host replied, "You are declining because the afterlife is different from the world. Here, there are many honors and resources, and my body and clothing are well cared for, which will be better than your hard work in the world. I very much desire to work together, and I hope you will lower your expectations to meet my expectations." Kuo again firmly declined, saying, "My children are still young, and if I accept this role, there will be no one to care for them. The affection between father and son is something that can be pitied." Kuo then wept and bowed. The host said, "Your refusal is so sincere, how can I force you? If I cannot persuade you, I deeply regret it."

From the table, a document was taken and used to detain someone. Afterward, Kuò expressed gratitude and declined to return. The host asked, "Do you not wish to pay respects to your deceased parents?" Then, a person was sent to lead Kuò away, passing through many temples and offices. They finally reached a wall, with the city gate and lintel aligned, which was a prison. Kuò was led inside and moved to a corner, where there were several buildings closely packed and connected, but in poor condition. In one of these buildings, Kuò saw his biological mother, Yang Shi, who was dressed poorly and looked very different from her usual self. She was surprised and delighted to see Kuò. Near the door, there was a person with visible injuries and a strange appearance who called out to Kuò. Kuò was startled and asked who she was.
